desc: Leafhopper assassin bug — slender, pale green-brown reduviid. Applies sticky resin from tibial glands to forelegs for prey capture — a 'flypaper trap.' Important garden predator of leafhoppers, caterpillars, and other pest insects. Can bite painfully in self-defense. Beneficial.

fm.Behavior: Ambush predator; sticky forelegs; captures flies, aphids

hp: Caterpillar predator. In gardens. Preys on caterpillars.

desc: Large, dark assassin bug attracted to lights. Painful bite — one of the most painful insect bites in SoCal. Not a kissing bug.

fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; flies to lights; painful defensive bite
fm.Habitat: Under bark, debris; enters homes at night

hp: Acoustic signaler — song/stridulation important in mate location. Prey for bats and birds. Western.

desc: North America's largest assassin bug. Distinctive cogwheel crest on thorax. Powerful predator. At western range edge in LA.

fm.Behavior: Ambush predator; slow; painful bite if handled
fm.Diet: Predator — caterpillars, beetles, other large insects

hp: Predatory hemipteran — captures other insects with raptorial forelegs. Largest.

desc: Bloodsucking assassin bug. Vector of Chagas disease (Trypanosoma cruzi). Bites sleeping humans at night. Found in woodrat nests.

fm.Color: Dark brown-black with orange-red lateral margins
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal blood feeder; bites face/lips of sleeping hosts; defecates near bite (disease transmission)
fm.Habitat: Woodrat nests, rock crevices; enters homes

hp: Chagas disease vector — Trypanosoma cruzi confirmed in SoCal triatomines (Shender et al. 2016, PLoS NTD). Nocturnal blood-feeder.

desc: Harlequin bug — spectacular orange-and-black pentatomid with bold checkered pattern. Major pest of Brassicaceae — cabbage, kale, broccoli, and mustard. Bright aposematic warning coloration advertises its foul taste and smell. Aggregates on plants. Year-round in SoCal.

fm.Behavior: Gregarious; sucks plant juices; warning coloration; bad smell
fm.Habitat: Gardens, crops — wherever cruciferous plants grow

hp: On brassicaceae (mustard family). Agricultural pest. On cabbage.

desc: Invasive from Asia. Shield-shaped brown with marbled pattern. Major agricultural pest. Aggregates in homes in fall.

fm.Behavior: Invasive; aggregates in buildings fall/winter; agricultural pest

hp: Brown marmorated stink bug — invasive from Asia causing $37M+ annual crop damage in eastern US (Leskey & Nielsen 2018). Under surveillance in SoCal.

desc: Green stink bug — bright green shield bug common on vegetables and fruit. Piercing-sucking mouthparts damage tomatoes and peppers. Strong defensive odor.

fm.Behavior: Plant feeder; strong defensive smell; damages fruit

hp: On vegetables, beans, tomatoes — piercing-sucking damage to fruit. Defensive stink glands.

desc: Say's green stink bug — bright green pentatomid with diagnostic pale lateral margins. Named for Thomas Say, father of American entomology. Extremely common on native plants. Feeds on developing seeds and fruit. Can damage tomatoes and other crops when abundant.

fm.Behavior: Plant feeder; aggregates on seed heads

hp: Plant sap feeder on various hosts. On tomato.

desc: Large plant bug with flattened hind tibiae ('leaf feet'). White zigzag band across wings. Major citrus/pomegranate pest.

fm.Color: Dark brown; white zigzag band on wings; flattened hind legs
fm.Behavior: Plant piercer; damages fruit; gregarious nymphs; strong flier
fm.Diet: Fruit and seeds — citrus, pomegranate, tomato

hp: Western leaf-footed bug — on citrus, pomegranate, tomato, and almonds. Piercing-sucking damage to developing fruit. Buzzy flight.

desc: Western leaffooted bug — brown coreid with expanded hind tibiae. Feeds on developing seeds and fruit. Common on almonds, pistachios, tomatoes. Aggregates in fall.
hp: Solanaceae associate — feeds on tomato, pepper, or nightshade family plants. Family Coreidae.

desc: Thomas's leaf-footed bug — large dark coreid (20-25mm) on agave, yucca, and prickly pear cactus. Powerfully built hind femora with pronounced spines. Feeds on plant sap and developing fruit. Produces pungent defensive odor. Males fence with hind legs in territorial disputes. Desert and foothill habitats.

fm.Color: Dark brown-black; robust; spiny hind femora
fm.Behavior: On agave flower stalks; slow; strong flier

hp: Cactus; agave; pine; yucca. Associated with Coastal Prickly Pear and Desert Agave.

desc: California bordered plant bug — dark pyrrhocorid with vivid orange border. Nymphs are spectacular: gregarious aggregations of metallic blue individuals. Striking at all life stages. On native plants. Feeds on plant sap. One of the most visually dramatic true bugs in LA County.

fm.Color: Adults: dark with orange margins. Nymphs: metallic blue (striking)
fm.Behavior: Gregarious; feeds on many plants; nymphs conspicuously blue

hp: Citrus associate — on orange, lemon, grapefruit (Rutaceae). Preys on ant. Metallic coloring.

desc: Small milkweed bug — red-orange and black seed bug on milkweed. Sequesters cardiac glycosides from milkweed seeds. Warning coloration deters predators.

fm.Color: Red-orange and black; X-pattern on wings
fm.Behavior: Gregarious on milkweed; toxic; warning coloration

hp: On Asclepias (milkweed). Aposematic — sequesters cardiac glycosides. Red-orange and black seed bug on milkweed. Sequesters cardia.

desc: Large milkweed bug — orange-banded on black. Classic aposematic (warning-colored) insect used extensively in biology research and education. Feeds on milkweed seeds, sequestering cardiac glycosides for chemical defense. Easy to rear — a standard teaching organism in entomology labs.

fm.Behavior: Gregarious on milkweed seed pods; toxic

hp: On milkweed — sequesters cardiac glycosides. On Asclepias (milkweed).

desc: Western boxelder bug — black with red-orange lines. Aggregates en masse on sun-warmed walls in fall, alarming homeowners with swarms of hundreds. Feeds on boxelder and maple seeds. Nuisance pest — enters buildings through gaps. Harmless. Produces foul odor if crushed.

fm.Behavior: Mass aggregations on warm walls; enters homes fall/winter

hp: Boxelder bug — feeds on Acer negundo (boxelder) and maple seeds. Aggregates on warm walls in fall. Nuisance — enters buildings. Harmless.

desc: Bagrada bug — invasive pentatomid from Africa, first detected in SoCal in 2008. Small (5-7mm) but devastating — black, white, and orange shield shape. Feeds on all Brassicaceae — decimates broccoli, kale, arugula, and native mustards. Rapidly spreading. Major emerging pest.

fm.Color: Black with white/orange markings; shield-shaped; small
fm.Behavior: Invasive; pierces plant tissue causing severe damage; spreading rapidly

hp: On brassicaceae (mustard family). Agricultural pest. On Brassicaceae (mustards).

desc: Walks on water surface using hydrophobic leg hairs. Detects prey vibrations through water. Common on any slow stream or pond.

fm.Behavior: Skates on water surface film; detects vibrations; predator
fm.Habitat: Streams, ponds, any calm water surface

hp: Stream and river habitats. Aquatic or semi-aquatic lifestyle. Family Gerridae.

desc: Bizarre treehopper with tall, laterally compressed thorn-like pronotum. Tropical species established in urban SoCal.

fm.Behavior: Gregarious on ornamental trees; females guard eggs

hp: On ornamental trees and shrubs. Thorn-mimicking sap feeder. Ant-tended for honeydew.

desc: Brilliant turquoise-blue with orange spots. One of the most photogenic insects in LA County. On coast live oak.

fm.Behavior: Gregarious on oak twigs; maternal care of eggs

hp: On coast live oak (quercus agrifolia). Associated with Coast Live Oak. On Quercus (oak). Family Membracidae.

desc: Large leafhopper. Vector of Pierce's disease — devastating to grapevines. Invasive from SE USA. Dark with clear wings.

fm.Behavior: Powerful flier; xylem feeder; excretes copious liquid; disease vector
fm.Diet: Xylem sap — polyphagous; vectors Xylella fastidiosa

hp: Plant/animal disease vector. Vectors Pierce's disease (Xylella fastidiosa) to grapevines. On grape.

desc: Blue-green sharpshooter — vivid blue-green and red-orange striped leafhopper. Vectors Pierce's disease (Xylella fastidiosa) to grapevines, but a native species (unlike the invasive glassy-winged sharpshooter). One of the most colorful leafhoppers in NA. On many plant species.

fm.Behavior: Xylem feeder; vectors Pierce's disease; on riparian vegetation

hp: Plant/animal disease vector. Vectors Pierce's disease (Xylella fastidiosa) to grapevines. On grape. Family Cicadellidae.

desc: Red-banded leafhopper — spectacular leafhopper with parallel red and blue-green stripes — one of the most colorful insects in LA gardens. On ornamental plants. Can vector Pierce's disease of grapevines (Xylella fastidiosa). Despite beauty, a potential pest. Very common.

fm.Color: Bright red and blue-green parallel stripes
fm.Behavior: Hops readily when disturbed; common on ornamental shrubs

hp: Viticulture associate — on grapevines (Vitis). Vineyard pest or resident. Vectors Pierce's disease (Xylella fastidiosa) to grapevines.

desc: Apache cicada — LA's most common large summer cicada. Males produce deafening continuous buzz from tree canopies on hot days — the soundtrack of SoCal summers. Desert-adapted — can thermoregulate by evaporative cooling. Nymphs develop underground 2-5 years feeding on tree roots.

fm.Sound: Loud continuous buzz on hot days (>95°F); can reach 90dB
fm.Behavior: Males sing from canopy; nymphs underground 2–5 years
fm.Habitat: Desert edge, urban trees — hot dry areas

hp: Desert and desert-edge habitats. Most Common.

desc: Putnam's cicada — small, dark cicada of mountain chaparral. Males produce quiet clicking sounds (not the loud buzz of Diceroprocta). Often goes unnoticed because the song is subtle. Named for entomologist F.W. Putnam. Multi-year underground nymphal development on shrub roots.

fm.Color: Dark brown-black; small for a cicada
fm.Behavior: Males click from branches; cryptic

hp: Root feeder on chaparral shrubs. Root-feeding nymph underground.

desc: Say's cicada — medium-sized dark cicada of foothill and mountain habitats. Males produce loud buzzy song from shrubs and trees. One of several Okanagana species.

fm.Behavior: Canopy singer; nymphs underground for years

hp: Root-feeding nymph on shrubs/trees for years underground. Adult male song from foothill chaparral.

desc: Consperse stink bug — mottled brown pentatomid. One of the most frequently encountered shield bugs in LA gardens. Agricultural pest of tomatoes, peppers, peaches, and almonds — piercing-sucking feeding causes dimpling and cat-facing damage on fruit. Overwinters in leaf litter.

fm.Color: Mottled brown with pale speckling; shield-shaped
fm.Behavior: Plant feeder; damages fruit; aggregates overwinter

hp: Consperse stink bug — agricultural pest of tomatoes, peppers, peaches, almonds. Piercing-sucking causes dimpling ('cat-facing') damage to fruit.

desc: Western tarnished plant bug — small, extremely variable mirid (brown, green, or reddish). One of the most economically important agricultural pests in western NA — damages cotton, alfalfa, strawberries, and many vegetables by puncturing developing fruit and flower buds. Difficult to control.

fm.Color: Variable — green, brown, reddish; V-mark on scutellum
fm.Behavior: Fast-moving; feeds on flower buds and developing fruit
fm.Diet: Flower buds, developing seeds — major crop pest

hp: On flowers. Part of flower-visiting community. On cotton. Family Miridae.

desc: Dark assassin bug with red-orange abdomen. Ambushes bees and other pollinators on flowers. Sticky resinous forelegs.

fm.Behavior: Ambushes bees on flowers; sticky resinous forelegs
fm.Diet: Predator — bees, flies, other flower visitors

hp: Citrus associate — on orange, lemon, grapefruit (Rutaceae). Family Reduviidae.

desc: Blood-sucking conenose — reddish-brown triatomine with orange lateral margins. Nocturnal blood-feeder on mammals. Can vector Chagas disease (Trypanosoma cruzi) — confirmed cases in SoCal, though transmission risk remains low. Bites cause allergic reactions in sensitized individuals. Desert edge.

fm.Behavior: Nocturnal blood feeder; Chagas disease vector

hp: Citrus associate — on orange, lemon, grapefruit (Rutaceae). Potential vector of Chagas disease (Trypanosoma cruzi).

desc: Squash bug — dark shield-shaped coreid and major pest of cucurbits. Eggs are distinctive — copper-colored, laid in neat rows on leaf undersides. Both nymphs and adults sap-feed, causing wilting ('Anasa wilt'). Overwinters as adult under debris. Very difficult to control in gardens.

fm.Behavior: Sucks plant juices; causes wilting; overwinters under debris
fm.Habitat: Gardens — wherever squash, pumpkin, cucumber grow

hp: Garden and crop pest. Herbivore on cultivated plants. On Fraxinus (ash).

desc: Red-shouldered bug — red-eyed soapberry bug with black-and-red coloring. Gregarious — forms conspicuous aggregations on golden rain tree (Koelreuteria) seeds. Originally on native Sapindaceae, rapidly adapted to ornamental rain trees. Classic case of host-switching evolution in real time.

fm.Color: Black with red eyes and red shoulders/margins
fm.Behavior: Mass aggregations on fallen seeds; harmless; conspicuous
fm.Diet: Seeds of Sapindaceae — golden rain tree, balloon vine

hp: Sap-feeding hemipteran on host plant tissue. Family Rhopalidae.

desc: Lunate leaf-footed bug — large brown coreid of desert creosote scrub. Distinctive crescent-shaped (lunate) expansions on hind tibiae. Feeds on creosote bush (Larrea) fruit and stems. One of the more specialized LA coreids — tied to desert scrub habitat.

fm.Color: Dark brown; crescent-shaped hind tibial lobes
fm.Behavior: On creosote bush; slow-moving

hp: Specialist on creosote bush (larrea tridentata). On Larrea (creosote). Feeds on creosote bush (Larrea) fruit and stems.

desc: Delicate green insect with golden eyes and lacy wings. Larvae ('aphid lions') are voracious predators. Commercially sold as biocontrol.

fm.Size: ½ in body; wings to 1 in
fm.Color: Bright green; golden eyes; delicate lacy wings
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; attracted to lights; larvae are 'aphid lions' — voracious predators

hp: Biocontrol agent; aphid predator. Specialist aphid predator — important biocontrol. Preys on aphid. Family Chrysopidae.

desc: Green stink bug — small green pentatomid common on many plants. Feeds on developing seeds and fruit. Often confused with other green shield bugs — requires examination of ventral surface and genitalia for definitive ID. Part of the large and difficult Banasa genus.

fm.Color: Green with narrow pale margins; shield-shaped
fm.Behavior: Plant feeder; common on tree foliage

hp: Plant sap feeder on various hosts.

desc: Predatory stink bug — one of the few. Prominent shoulder spines. Commercially used for biocontrol of pest caterpillars.

fm.Color: Brown with dark speckles; prominent shoulder spines
fm.Behavior: Predator — impales caterpillars and beetle larvae with beak

hp: Biocontrol agent; pine; caterpillar predator. Preys on caterpillars.

desc: Toad bug — bizarre toad-shaped bug of stream and pond margins. Bumpy, warty texture and bulging compound eyes convincingly mimic a tiny toad. Jumps to catch small arthropod prey on muddy banks. One of the most morphologically unusual true bugs in LA County.

fm.Color: Mottled gray-brown; bumpy; toad-like; bulging eyes
fm.Behavior: Jumps like a toad to catch prey; sits on mud/sand near water
fm.Habitat: Stream margins, pond edges — on wet sand/mud

hp: Stream and river habitats. Aquatic or semi-aquatic lifestyle. Tiny.

desc: Conchuela stink bug — dark brown pentatomid with distinctive orange or red border and connexivum. Significant agricultural pest of beans, tomatoes, and cotton. Distinguished from C. sayi by dark brown (not green) ground color with orange trim. Strong flier.

fm.Shape: Dark brown stink bug with orange/red border

hp: Cotton pest — feeds on cotton bolls, leaves, or squares. On tomato.

desc: Insidious flower bug — tiny (2-3mm) predatory anthocorid. Voracious consumer of thrips, spider mites, and aphid eggs. One of the most important biological control agents in agriculture — commercially reared for greenhouse release. Can bite humans — surprisingly painful for such a tiny insect.

fm.Shape: Tiny predator — eats thrips, mites, aphid eggs

hp: Biocontrol agent; aphid predator. Specialist aphid predator — important biocontrol. Preys on aphid. Tiny.

desc: Wickham's stilt bug — delicate, extremely long-legged berytid on vegetation. Predatory — feeds on small insects, mites, and insect eggs. Important beneficial bug in gardens and crops. Slender build and slow deliberate movement. Named for early collector H.F. Wickham.
hp: Generalist insect predator. Beneficial in gardens. Preys on ant. Family Berytidae.

desc: Giant water bug — massive aquatic predator reaching 65mm. Ambushes fish, frogs, and large aquatic invertebrates with powerful raptorial forelegs. Delivers painful bite — earned nickname 'toe-biter.' Attracted to lights at night ('electric light bug'). Strong flier between water bodies.
hp: Predatory hemipteran — captures other insects with raptorial forelegs. Preys on ant.

desc: Pale green assassin bug — light green reduviid lurking on flowers and foliage. Ambush predator of pollinators and other flower-visiting insects. Sticky forelegs trap prey. Important garden predator. Can deliver a painful bite if handled carelessly. Native beneficial insect.

fm.Shape: Pale green predatory bug lurking on flowers

hp: Predatory; pollinator. In gardens.

desc: Bordered plant bug — large dark bug with orange-red border. Nymphs brilliant metallic blue. Gregarious on various plants. One of the most photogenic true bugs.

fm.Color: Dark with orange-red margins; nymphs metallic blue

hp: Sap feeder on various native plants. Nymphs gregarious — metallic blue aggregations on stems.

desc: Western conifer seed bug — large (18-20mm) brown coreid with distinctive flattened hind tibiae ('leaf-footed'). Feeds on developing conifer seeds. Enters homes en masse in fall seeking overwintering shelter. Loud buzzy flight. Harmless but alarming when found indoors. Now invasive in Europe.

fm.Shape: Large brown bug with flattened hind legs

hp: Found in and around buildings and structures.

desc: Opuntia bug — brown coreid feeding on prickly pear cactus pads. Causes yellowing, scarring, and stippling damage to Opuntia. Can weaken plants but rarely kills them. Both nymphs and adults feed on cactus. An important herbivore in cactus ecology — controls Opuntia density.

fm.Shape: Brown bug feeding on prickly pear pads

hp: Specialist on cactus (cactus spp.). On Opuntia (cactus). Family Coreidae.

desc: Brown water scorpion — stick-like aquatic bug with long rear breathing tube (siphon). Ambush predator — grabs tadpoles, small fish, and aquatic insects with raptorial forelegs. Not a scorpion — the siphon is a snorkel, not a stinger. In still ponds and slow streams.

fm.Habitat: Stick-like aquatic bug with long breathing tube

hp: Predatory hemipteran — captures other insects with raptorial forelegs. Family Nepidae.

desc: Pacific ash plant bug — small green mirid causing leaf stippling on ash trees. Can cause significant cosmetic damage to ornamental ash (Fraxinus) in heavy infestations — leaves become speckled and may drop prematurely. Most damaging in warm years. Native.

fm.Shape: Small plant bug causing leaf stippling on ash trees

hp: Specialist on ash trees (Fraxinus) — causes leaf stippling damage to ornamental ashes.

desc: Elm seed bug — small dark seed bug recently established in SoCal from Europe (first NA record 2012). Aggregates in enormous numbers on buildings in fall seeking overwintering shelter. On sycamore and boxelder seeds. Nuisance pest in homes but harmless. Rapidly expanding range.
hp: On western sycamore (platanus racemosa). On Platanus (sycamore). Enormous.

desc: Four-humped stink bug — bark-colored pentatomid perfectly camouflaged on tree trunks. Mottled gray-brown with four small pronotal bumps. Feeds on tree sap. Virtually invisible against oak bark. One of the best-camouflaged bugs in LA — flattened body hugs bark contours.

fm.Color: Bark-brown — excellent camouflage on tree trunks

hp: On oak bark. Part of bark-dwelling arthropod community. On Quercus (oak). Flattened body.

desc: Pale water boatman — small aquatic hemipteran swimming with oar-like hind legs. Unlike backswimmers, swims right-side-up. Herbivorous — feeds on algae scraped from pond bottoms. Important fish food — a key link in freshwater food webs. Can fly between water bodies.

fm.Habitat: Small aquatic bug swimming with oar-like legs

hp: Aquatic larvae — important fish food. Preys on ant. Family Corixidae.

desc: Wavy backswimmer — aquatic hemipteran that swims upside-down using long hind legs as oars. Air bubble trapped on ventral surface provides buoyancy and oxygen. Voracious predator of mosquito larvae, tadpoles, and small fish. Can deliver a surprisingly painful bite — handle carefully.

fm.Shape: Swims upside down using long hind legs as oars

hp: Predator of mosquito larvae — natural mosquito biocontrol. Preys on mosquito. Aquatic hemipteran that swims upside-down using long hind le.

desc: Flat bug — extremely flattened aradid living under bark of dead trees. Feeds on fungal hyphae in dead wood — one of the few heteropteran families with a fungal diet. Bark-matching brown coloring. Body so flat it can hide in crevices thinner than a dime.

fm.Shape: Extremely flat bug living under bark

hp: On tree bark. Part of bark-dwelling community. Flattened body.

desc: American ambush bug — tiny (8-12mm) cryptic bug hiding in flower heads. Catches insects many times its own size — including bees, butterflies, and wasps — with enormously powerful raptorial forelegs. One of the most remarkably strong predators relative to body size in the insect world.

fm.Behavior: Catches insects much larger than itself

hp: Generalist predator of insects and other arthropods.

desc: Pacific brown lacewing — small brown neuropteran (Order Neuroptera, not Hemiptera despite being grouped here). Both adults and larvae eat aphids — important biological control. Delicate lace-like wings. Nocturnal. Distinguished from green lacewings by brown color and smaller size.

fm.Shape: Small brown neuropteran (not a true bug)

hp: Biological control agent — predator of aphids. Specialist aphid predator — important biocontrol. Preys on aphid. Family Hemerobiidae.

desc: Beautiful cicada — small dark cicada of mountain pine-oak forests. Males sing from treetops with distinctive song pattern. Multi-year underground nymphal development feeding on tree roots. Named 'bella' (beautiful) for the subtle wing patterning. Mountain habitats above 4,000 ft.

fm.Habitat: Small dark cicada of mountain forests

hp: Root-feeding nymph underground on oak and pine roots for years. Adult male sings from mountain trees. Mountain forest cicada.

desc: Sycamore lace bug — tiny tingid with extraordinarily ornate, lace-like wing venation. Feeds on sycamore leaf undersides causing white stippling damage visible from above. Can partially defoliate trees in severe years. The delicate wing lace pattern is remarkably beautiful under magnification.

fm.Behavior: Causes stippling damage; can defoliate

hp: On western sycamore (platanus racemosa). On Platanus (sycamore). Defoliator.

desc: Common bed bug — obligate human blood-feeder. Extremely flattened body hides in mattress seams, headboard cracks, and furniture joints by day. Night feeder — attracted by body heat and CO2. Major global resurgence since early 2000s due to insecticide resistance. Does not transmit disease.

fm.Behavior: Nocturnal blood-feeder; resurgent since 2000s

hp: Obligate human blood parasite. Nocturnal feeder hiding in mattress seams. Flattened body.

desc: Two-spotted stink bug — predatory pentatomid with red-and-black coloring. Specialist predator of Colorado potato beetle larvae — grasps and sucks them dry. Important biological control agent. Bold aposematic coloring warns predators of its own chemical defenses.

fm.Shape: Predatory stink bug with red and black coloring

hp: Biocontrol agent; predatory. Specialist stink bug predator.

desc: Sweetpotato whitefly — tiny sap-feeding pest with enormous agricultural impact. Vectors over 100 plant viruses including tomato yellow leaf curl. Develops resistance to insecticides rapidly. Biotype B (silverleaf whitefly) is especially damaging. Billions in annual crop losses worldwide.
hp: Solanaceae associate — feeds on tomato, pepper, or nightshade family plants. Tiny.

desc: Citrus mealybug — white waxy-coated scale insect on citrus, grapes, and ornamentals. Produces copious honeydew promoting sooty mold growth. Vectors grapevine leafroll virus. Controlled by the parasitoid wasp Leptomastix dactylopii — another California biocontrol success story.
hp: Citrus; biocontrol agent; parasitoid of wasps. On Citrus (citrus). Family Pseudococcidae.

desc: California red scale — circular armored scale on citrus bark, leaves, and fruit. One of the most important citrus pests worldwide. Female permanently attached under waxy cover. Controlled by parasitoid wasp Aphytis melinus — a classic biological control success story in California agriculture.
hp: Citrus; biocontrol agent; parasitoid of wasps. On Citrus (citrus). Family Diaspididae.

desc: Giant bark aphid — the largest aphid in North America at 6mm. On oak, sycamore, and pecan bark. Ant-tended — ants protect aphids in exchange for honeydew. When threatened, adults kick defensively with hind legs. Impressive size makes it visible without magnification — unusual for an aphid.

fm.Size: ⅛ in–¼ in — largest NA aphid

hp: On Platanus racemosa (western sycamore). Causes leaf stippling. Ant-tended — mutualistic honeydew exchange.

desc: Bright yellow aphid on milkweed and oleander. Sequesters cardiac glycosides — toxic to predators. Cosmopolitan.
hp: On Asclepias (milkweed). Aposematic — sequesters cardiac glycosides. Toxic to predators. Cosmopolitan.

desc: Citricola scale — soft brown hemispherical scale on citrus. Major pest producing copious honeydew that promotes black sooty mold on leaves and fruit. Important target for biological control using parasitoid wasps (Metaphycus). Part of LA's significant citrus pest complex.
hp: Citrus; biocontrol agent; parasitoid of wasps. On Citrus (citrus). Family Coccidae.

desc: Cochineal scale — white cottony mass on Opuntia cactus hiding a tiny hemipteran that produces brilliant carmine red dye. Historically one of the most valuable trade commodities in the Americas — worth more than gold by weight. Still used in food coloring and cosmetics (E120).

fm.Color: Dark red body under white cochineal wax

hp: Specialist on cactus (cactus spp.). On Opuntia (cactus). Tiny.

desc: Small green aphid — most important plant virus vector worldwide. On peach (primary) and many crops (secondary).
hp: Aphid predator. Important biocontrol agent. Specialist aphid predator — important biocontrol. On peach.

desc: White cottony scale on citrus. Australian invasive that triggered first classical biocontrol success (Vedalia beetle 1889).

fm.Size: ⅛ in–⅓ in with white egg sac
fm.Color: Orange-brown body + white fluted egg mass

hp: Sap feeder on Citrus. Produces honeydew promoting sooty mold. Agricultural pest. On Citrus (citrus). Family Monophlebidae.

desc: Asian citrus psyllid — tiny mottled brown psyllid. Vectors huanglongbing (citrus greening disease, HLB) — the most devastating citrus disease globally, with no cure. Infected trees produce bitter, misshapen fruit and die within years. CDFA quarantine pest under active eradication.
hp: Vectors HLB/citrus greening — most devastating citrus disease globally (Grafton-Cardwell et al. 2013). CDFA quarantine pest.

desc: Citrus thrips — tiny yellow thysanopteran that scars citrus fruit rind during the expanding phase, creating ring-like cosmetic damage that downgrades fresh-market fruit. California's most economically significant citrus pest. Rasping mouthparts feed on epidermal cells. Multiple generations per year.
hp: Citrus thrips — rasps citrus fruit rind during growth, causing ring-scarring. Most important citrus pest in CA (Morse & Hoddle 2006).

desc: Greenhouse whitefly — tiny white-winged sap-feeder. Clouds of adults fly up when plants are disturbed. Major greenhouse and garden pest worldwide. Produces honeydew promoting sooty mold. Develops insecticide resistance rapidly. Controlled by parasitoid Encarsia formosa.
hp: Predator or parasitoid of whiteflies — biocontrol of crop pests.

desc: Veiny barklouse — small brown psocid living in herds on tree bark. Not a true louse — feeds on lichens, algae, and bark microflora. Harmless to trees and humans. Often found in groups of 20-50 on oak trunks. The gregarious herding behavior is distinctive and sometimes alarming to homeowners.

fm.Habitat: Tree bark — herds visible on trunks

hp: Oak; lichen. Associated with Coast Live Oak.

desc: Eastern boxelder bug — black with three red lines on pronotum. Aggregates by the thousands on warm sun-facing walls in fall and spring. Feeds on boxelder (Acer negundo) seeds. Nuisance pest in homes but completely harmless. Occasionally reaches SoCal from eastern populations.
hp: Ground-dwelling seed feeder — on fallen seeds under litter. Seed predation affects plant regeneration. Family Rhopalidae.

desc: Van Duzee's cicada — small dark cicada of inland valleys and oak woodland. Males sing from shrubs and trees. Multi-year underground development (4-7 years as root-feeding nymph). Named for E.P. Van Duzee, prolific hemipterist. Part of the endemic western cicada fauna.
hp: On oak roots underground. Associated with Coast Live Oak.

desc: Delicate green insect with golden eyes (technically Neuroptera). Larvae voracious aphid lions. Sold as biocontrol.
hp: Biological control agent — predator of aphids. Specialist aphid predator — important biocontrol. Preys on aphid. Family Chrysopidae.

desc: Short-horned antlion — adult is a delicate lacewing-like insect. Larva is the famous 'doodlebug' that digs conical sand pit traps to capture ants — one of the most remarkable predation strategies in the insect world. Desert and sandy habitats. Adults nocturnal, attracted to lights.
hp: LA County true bug — habitat-specific ecological role.

desc: Western flower thrips — tiny (1-2mm) slender thysanopteran. One of the most damaging agricultural pests worldwide — feeds on flowers and fruit, vectors tomato spotted wilt virus. Rasping-sucking mouthparts cause silvery scarring. Extremely difficult to control. Develops insecticide resistance rapidly.
hp: Ambushes prey on flowers. Preys on insects. On tomato.

desc: Invasive from Asia — not yet established in CA but under active quarantine. Adults spectacular: gray forewings with black spots, red hindwings. Report sightings to CDFA.

fm.Color: Gray FW with black spots; red HW
fm.Status: QUARANTINE PEST — report sightings to CDFA

hp: Spotted lanternfly — quarantine pest. Not established in CA. Feeds on 70+ hosts including grape (Dara et al. 2015). Active CDFA surveillance.

desc: Green thorn-shaped treehopper — diagnostic triangular pronotum. Feeds on plant sap. Female cuts crescent-shaped egg slits in bark. Common on alfalfa, weeds, and ornamentals.
hp: On tree bark. Part of bark-dwelling community. On alfalfa.

desc: Invasive Australian psyllid devastating eucalyptus throughout LA since 1998. Nymphs build conspicuous white crystalline 'lerps' (sugar shelters) on leaves. Can defoliate trees.

fm.Habitat: Eucalyptus — white crystalline lerps on leaves diagnostic
fm.Damage: Defoliates eucalyptus; honeydew and sooty mold

hp: On eucalyptus — introduced with host tree. On Eucalyptus (eucalyptus). Invasive Australian psyllid devastating eucalyptus throughou.

desc: Slender tan predatory bug. Important biological control agent — feeds on aphids, leafhoppers, caterpillars, and mites in agricultural fields. Resembles a thin assassin bug.

fm.Role: Biological control — feeds on aphids, leafhoppers, caterpillars

hp: Biocontrol agent; aphid predator. Specialist aphid predator — important biocontrol. Preys on aphid. Family Nabidae.

desc: Invasive Mexican whitefly — produces spectacular long white waxy filaments hanging from leaf undersides. Common on hibiscus, banana, mulberry. Arrived in LA ~1992.

fm.Size: ⅛ in body; filaments up to 2 in
fm.Color: White; produces long waxy filaments hanging from leaves
fm.Habitat: Hibiscus, banana, mulberry — conspicuous white filaments

hp: On ornamentals — ficus, hibiscus, citrus. Produces long waxy filaments and copious honeydew.

desc: Bright yellow-green with 4 black stripes. Feeds on over 250 plant species. Causes distinctive round necrotic spots on leaves. Nymph is bright red.

fm.Damage: Round necrotic leaf spots at feeding sites

hp: Plant sap feeder on various hosts. Preys on ant. Family Miridae.

desc: Bright green sharpshooter with pointed head. Vectors Pierce's disease of grapevines (Xylella fastidiosa). Common in irrigated areas, lawns, and riparian zones.

fm.Habitat: Irrigated lawns, riparian — common vector of Xylella

hp: Riparian corridor habitats. Vectors Pierce's disease (Xylella fastidiosa) to grapevines.

desc: Small waxy-white planthopper. Nymphs produce conspicuous white waxy filaments. Polyphagous on citrus, ornamentals. Introduced from eastern US.

fm.Habitat: Citrus, ornamentals — nymphs produce white wax

hp: On Asteraceae (composites) — daisies, sunflowers, sagebrush. On Citrus (citrus).

desc: Shiny black aphid on citrus new growth. Vectors Citrus Tristeza Virus. Dense colonies on tender shoots cause leaf curling. Managed by parasitoid wasps and lady beetles.
hp: Sap feeder on Citrus. Produces honeydew promoting sooty mold. Agricultural pest. On Citrus (citrus). Parasitoid. Black.

desc: Small brown froghopper — nymphs produce distinctive white frothy 'spittle' masses on plant stems. The familiar foamy blobs on garden plants. Adults jump powerfully.

fm.Color: Variable — tan, brown, or black; mottled
fm.Habitat: Garden plants — nymphal spittle masses on stems diagnostic
fm.Behavior: Nymphs produce protective foam; adults jump far

hp: Spittlebug — nymphs produce frothy protective spittle masses on plant stems.

desc: One of the largest true bugs in North America — up to 1.5 in. Beautiful: dark with orange-red margins. Nymphs are brilliant metallic blue-green. Desert species on mesquite.

fm.Color: Dark with orange-red margins; nymphs metallic blue-green

hp: On mesquite (prosopis) in desert habitat. On Prosopis (mesquite). Metallic coloring (5 in).

desc: Thread-legged assassin bug — one of the most bizarre insects in LA County. Extremely slender, stick-like body. Steals prey from spider webs — enters web without triggering spider, plucks trapped insects free. Fragile appearance belies effective predatory lifestyle. Nocturnal.
hp: Builds web to capture insects and spiders. Nocturnal.

desc: Colonial aphid producing white woolly wax. Dense colonies on apple, pyracantha, elm. Cottony masses diagnostic.
hp: Colonial sap feeder producing honeydew. On apple.

desc: Bright orange-red assassin bug. Sticky legs trap prey. Important garden predator. Common on milkweed and throughout gardens.
hp: On Asclepias (milkweed). Aposematic — sequesters cardiac glycosides.

desc: Tiny bug with intricate lace-like wings. Feeds on undersides of sycamore and alder leaves. Beautiful under magnification.
hp: On Platanus racemosa (western sycamore). Causes leaf stippling.

desc: Small green-yellow aphid on cotton, cucurbits, and ornamentals. Vectors many plant viruses. One of the most polyphagous aphids.
hp: Aphid predator. Important biocontrol agent. Specialist aphid predator — important biocontrol. On cotton.

desc: Large green or pink aphid on roses. Very common garden pest. Dense colonies on buds and new growth. Managed by ladybugs and lacewings.
hp: Rose aphid — colonial sap-feeder on Rosa. Produces honeydew. Ant-tended.

desc: Handsome planthopper — small pale green flatid planthopper. Sits motionless on stems in wedge-shaped resting posture. Common on many ornamental and native plants. Produces small flocculent waxy filaments. Distinguished from similar planthoppers by the clean pale green coloring.
hp: Sap feeder on various plants. Wedge-shaped, leaf-mimicking. Produces flocculent waxy filaments.

desc: Small dark treehopper with pronotal horn. Lives in species complex — different host-plant races. Vibrational communication through stems.

fm.Color: Dark with pale markings; pronotal horn

hp: Treehopper species complex — host-plant races diverging via vibrational communication differences.

desc: Mediterranean red bug — striking red-and-black seed bug recently established in SoCal (~2009). Forms dense aggregations on malvaceous plants. Feeds on seeds. Expanding range.
hp: Sap-feeding hemipteran on host plant tissue. Family Pyrrhocoridae.

desc: Southern green stink bug — bright green shield bug. One of the world's most important crop pests. Strong defensive odor. Feeds on over 30 crop families. Recently arrived in LA.
hp: Sap-feeding hemipteran on host plant tissue. Preys on ant. Family Pentatomidae.

desc: Torpedo bug — bright green planthopper with pointed head. Introduced from Australia. Wedge-shaped, flattened. Jumps powerfully. Common on ornamentals and citrus.
hp: Torpedo bug — pale green planthopper on citrus and ornamentals. Introduced from Australia. Distinctive torpedo-shaped body. Honeydew producer.

desc: Notched giant water bug — large aquatic predator. Males carry eggs on back (paternal care — rare in insects). Catches fish, tadpoles, aquatic insects. Painful bite if handled.

fm.Behavior: ♂ carries eggs on back — paternal care

hp: Predatory hemipteran — captures other insects with raptorial forelegs. Preys on ant.

desc: Broken-backed bug — common green plant bug. Named for the broken crossband on pronotum. Feeds on many plants. Very common in gardens and agricultural fields.

fm.Color: Green with broken pale band on pronotum

hp: Sap-feeding hemipteran on host plant tissue. Preys on ant. Family Miridae. Feeds on many plants.

desc: California bee assassin — large red-and-black assassin bug specializing on bees. Ambushes pollinators on flowers using sticky resin on forelegs. Native and ecologically important.

fm.Behavior: Ambushes bees on flowers with sticky legs

hp: Acoustic signaler — song/stridulation important in mate location. Prey for bats and birds.

desc: Potato mirid — green plant bug with dark punctures on pronotum. Introduced from Europe. Common pest of potatoes, legumes, and many garden plants.
hp: Garden and crop pest. Herbivore on cultivated plants. On potato. Family Miridae.

desc: Hyaline grass bug — small pale rhopalid bug on grasses and weedy plants. Very common in disturbed habitats. Congregates on drying weedy vegetation in summer.
hp: On grasses and forbs in grassland. On Poaceae (grasses). Family Rhopalidae.

desc: Cabbage aphid — gray-green waxy aphid forming dense colonies on brassica crops. Covered in powdery grayish wax (unlike green peach aphid). Major cole crop pest.

fm.Habitat: Cabbage, broccoli, kale — dense waxy colonies

hp: Aphid predator. Important biocontrol agent. Specialist aphid predator — important biocontrol. On cabbage.

desc: Crown whitefly — tiny moth-like insect on undersides of oak leaves. Nymphs produce distinctive crown-shaped waxy fringe. Common on coast live oak.

fm.Color: White; moth-like; crown-shaped nymphal wax on oak

hp: On coast live oak (quercus agrifolia). Associated with Coast Live Oak. On Quercus (oak). Tiny.

desc: Four-spined assassin bug — pale green assassin bug with four pronotal spines. Important predator of pest insects in gardens and agricultural fields. Can bite if handled.
hp: Four-spined assassin bug — predator in pine woodland and gardens. Captures caterpillars, beetles, and other insects. Beneficial predator.

desc: Cluster stink bug — introduced African stink bug recently established in SoCal (~2013). Yellow-orange with black punctures. Aggregates on various plants. Expanding range.
hp: Invasive from Africa (~2013). On Asteraceae and various crops. Aggregates on plants.

desc: Spring fishfly — large dark neuropteran (dobsonfly relative). Larvae aquatic in streams. Adults attracted to lights near water. Long filiform antennae (vs. pectinate in Corydalus).
hp: Freshwater aquatic habitat — streams and ponds.

desc: Sage leafhopper — tiny cicadellid feeding on sage, rosemary, and other Lamiaceae. Causes white stippling damage. Introduced from Mediterranean. Pest of herb gardens.

fm.Damage: White stippling on sage and rosemary leaves

hp: On Rosa (roses) — garden and wild species. On Rosa (rose). Tiny.

desc: Manzanita gall aphid — tiny aphid inducing distinctive red-purple leaf-fold galls on manzanita (Arctostaphylos). Very common in chaparral. Galls are diagnostic.

fm.Color: Dark; galls are red-purple leaf folds on manzanita

hp: On Arctostaphylos (manzanita) in chaparral. Native shrub specialist. Tiny. Tiny aphid inducing distinctive red-purple leaf-fold galls o.

desc: Milkweed seed bug — small dark seed bug on milkweed. Similar to large milkweed bug but smaller and darker. Feeds on milkweed seeds in pods.
hp: On Asclepias (milkweed). Aposematic — sequesters cardiac glycosides.

desc: Brilliant plant bug — small attractive mirid with metallic blue-green sheen. Predatory — feeds on mites and small insects on plants. Beneficial.
hp: Generalist predator of insects and other arthropods. Preys on ant. Metallic coloring.

desc: Expanded plant bug — small pale mirid on various plants. Common in gardens and weedy areas. Part of the very diverse plant bug family (Miridae).
hp: Sap-feeding hemipteran on host plant tissue. Preys on ant. Family Miridae. Solanaceous.

desc: Peppertree psyllid — tiny psyllid specialist on California pepper tree (Schinus molle). Forms pit galls on leaves. Very common wherever pepper trees grow.

fm.Color: Tiny; pit galls on pepper tree leaves

hp: Specialist on California pepper tree (Schinus molle). Forms pit galls on leaves.

desc: Whitecrossed seed bug — dark seed bug with white X-pattern on back. Common on ragwort and other Asteraceae. Conspicuous aggregations on host plants.

fm.Color: Dark with white cross on back

hp: LA County true bug — habitat-specific ecological role. On Asteraceae (composites).

desc: Pacific ambush bug — tiny cryptic predator hiding in flower heads. Raptorial forelegs grab prey many times its size — catches butterflies, bees, even wasps.

fm.Behavior: Catches prey much larger than itself

hp: Flower-ambush predator — captures pollinating insects on flower heads. Cryptic.

desc: Thirteen-spotted leafhopper — small green leafhopper with distinctive spotted pattern. On various trees and shrubs. One of the more recognizable leafhoppers.
hp: On various trees. Thirteen-spotted pattern distinctive. Part of canopy leafhopper community.

desc: Adventive psyllid — introduced psyllid on various ornamental plants. Forms galls on new growth. Part of the growing guild of invasive psyllids in SoCal.

fm.Color: Pale green; galls on new growth

hp: Introduced psyllid on ornamental plants. Induces leaf galls and distortion on new growth.

desc: Two-spotted leafhopper — tiny green leafhopper with two dark spots. Introduced from Asia. Pest of many ornamental plants. Vectors phytoplasma diseases.

fm.Color: Pale green with two dark spots

hp: Plant/animal disease vector. Agricultural/garden pest.

desc: Protracta leafhopper — tiny dark leafhopper on woody plants. Named for its protruding head. Part of the diverse leafhopper fauna.

fm.Color: Dark; resembles a fly speck on bark

hp: On woody plants — part of the chaparral leafhopper community. Potential phytoplasma vector.

desc: Saturn seed bug — distinctive seed bug with pale ring markings. On various plants. Mediterranean introduction. Spreading in SoCal.
hp: LA County true bug — habitat-specific ecological role. Family Lygaeidae.

desc: Spotted gum lerp psyllid — tiny psyllid on eucalyptus producing white crystalline 'lerps' (waxy covers). Introduced from Australia. Very common on eucalyptus.

fm.Color: Tiny; white crystalline lerps on eucalyptus

hp: On eucalyptus — introduced with host tree. On Eucalyptus (eucalyptus). Tiny.

desc: Dark plant bug — small dark mirid on various plants. Very common but overlooked. Part of the hyperdiverse plant bug family.
hp: On various native and ornamental plants. Omnivorous mirid — feeds on sap and small arthropods.

desc: Privet leafhopper — small green leafhopper on privet and olive. Vectors phytoplasma diseases. Very common on ornamental privet hedges.
hp: Privet leafhopper — vectors phytoplasma diseases to many ornamental and crop plants. On privet, ornamentals. Important disease vector.

desc: Cotton seed bug — small dark bug with translucent wings. Introduced from Old World. Pest of cotton and malvaceous plants. Recently established in SoCal.
hp: Cotton pest — feeds on cotton bolls, leaves, or squares. Family Oxycarenidae.

desc: Spiny-collared assassin bug — orange-brown assassin bug with spiny pronotum. Predatory on garden insects. Common on vegetation. Can bite if handled.
hp: Generalist insect predator. Beneficial in gardens. Spiny/armed.

desc: Four-spotted leafhopper — small green leafhopper with four dark spots on wings. On various plants in gardens and chaparral.

fm.Color: Green with four dark wing spots

hp: Chaparral sap feeder — on native shrubs. Part of native plant-insect community. Family Cicadellidae. Sycamore.

desc: Smoke tree sharpshooter — large leafhopper with mottled wings. Native. Similar to glassy-winged sharpshooter. On smoke tree and other desert plants.

fm.Color: Mottled brown; large for a leafhopper

hp: Desert and desert-edge habitats.

desc: Margined plant bug — small mirid with dark margins. On various plants in chaparral and gardens. Plant sap feeder.
hp: Sap-feeding hemipteran on host plant tissue. Preys on ant. Chaparral. Plant sap feeder.

desc: Red assassin bug — striking red-and-black assassin bug. Predatory — catches caterpillars and other insects. Common in chaparral. Can deliver painful defensive bite.
hp: Caterpillar predator. Chaparral. Preys on caterpillars.

desc: Consort leafhopper — small green-yellow leafhopper on oaks and other hardwood trees. Common in woodland canopy. Feeds on mesophyll cell contents. Part of the extremely diverse canopy leafhopper community — individual oak trees may host 20+ leafhopper species.
hp: On coast live oak (quercus agrifolia). On Quercus (oak). Woodland.

desc: Corn leaf aphid — small dark aphid colonizing corn and grain leaves. Can build enormous colonies. Important crop pest. Honeydew attracts sooty mold.

fm.Color: Dark blue-green; colonial on corn/grain leaves

hp: Stored product pest of grain and dried foods. On corn.

desc: Black-striped planthopper — small green planthopper with dark stripes. On grasses and sedges. Jumps powerfully when disturbed.
hp: On grasses and forbs in grassland. On Poaceae (grasses). Family Delphacidae.

desc: Pyriform scale — small pear-shaped soft scale on various ornamentals. Produces honeydew attracting sooty mold. Can damage landscape plants.
hp: Sessile sap feeder — honeydew producer.

desc: Clear-winged bark louse — small psocid on tree bark and dead branches. Feeds on fungi and algae. Forms groups. Harmless.
hp: Mycetophagous — feeds on fungi. Fungal associate — see lafungi.org. Family Psocidae.

desc: Fire spittlebug — small black froghopper with red-orange markings. Nymphs produce frothy spittle on grass stems. Named for fire-like coloring. Native.
hp: Citrus associate — on orange, lemon, grapefruit (Rutaceae). Family Cercopidae.

desc: California pepper tree psyllid — tiny psyllid inducing pit galls on California pepper tree (Schinus molle) leaves. Very common wherever pepper trees grow in LA.

fm.Color: Tiny; pit galls on pepper tree leaves

hp: Specialist on California pepper tree — induces pit galls on leaf tissue. Native psyllid.

desc: Cotton fleahopper — small pale green mirid. Pest of cotton — feeds on developing flower buds causing square loss. Common on various weedy plants.
hp: On flowers. Part of flower-visiting community. On cotton. Family Miridae.

desc: Cactus coreid — leaf-footed bug specializing on prickly pear cactus fruit. Flattened hind tibiae. Common wherever Opuntia grows. Native.
hp: Specialist on cactus (cactus spp.). On Opuntia (cactus). Flattened body.

desc: Potato aphid — large green or pink aphid on potatoes, tomatoes, and many plants. Long cornicles. Vectors many plant viruses. Very common garden pest.

fm.Color: Green or pink; long dark cornicles

hp: Corn (Zea mays) associate — larvae feed on roots, silk, or kernels. On tomato.

desc: Tip-marked coreid — brown leaf-footed bug on various plants. Expanded hind tibiae. Common on prickly pear and other plants in foothill habitats.
hp: On prickly pear cactus and other plants. Sap feeder with expanded hind tibiae (leaf-footed).

desc: Western sycamore lace bug — tiny bug with intricate lace-patterned wings. Feeds on sycamore leaf undersides. Causes stippling damage. Beautiful under magnification.
hp: On western sycamore (platanus racemosa). On Platanus (sycamore). Tiny.

desc: Two-spined leafhopper — green sharpshooter with two spiny projections on vertex. Vectors Xylella. Common on oaks and riparian plants.
hp: Plant disease vector — transmits pathogens via piercing-sucking feeding. On Quercus (oak).

desc: Red-banded sharpshooter — colorful leafhopper with red and green banding. Common on ornamentals. One of several Graphocephala sharpshooters in LA.
hp: On ornamentals. Potential vector of Xylella fastidiosa (Pierce's disease) to grapevines.

desc: Willow leafhopper — green sharpshooter found on willows along riparian corridors. Similar to other Graphocephala but host-specific.
hp: Leafhopper — xylem/phloem sap feeder. Part of canopy insect community. On Salix (willow).

desc: Charcoal seed bug — dark gray seed bug common in weedy areas. Aggregates in large numbers. Feeds on seeds of various plants. Often found under debris.
hp: Sap-feeding hemipteran on host plant tissue.

desc: Uhler's stink bug — green shield bug similar to green stink bug but with white-tipped spine on each shoulder. Common on various plants. Defensive stink odor.
hp: In conifer forests (pinus). Associated with Coulter Pine. On Pinus (pine). Family Pentatomidae.

desc: Van Duzee's cicada — medium-dark cicada of oak woodland. Males produce loud buzzing song. Common in foothill canyons. Named for hemipterist E.P. Van Duzee.

fm.Color: Dark; loud buzzing song in oaks

hp: On coast live oak (quercus agrifolia). Associated with Coast Live Oak. On Quercus (oak). Woodland. Van.

desc: Versute sharpshooter — medium green leafhopper. Vectors Pierce's disease of grapevines. Common on many ornamental and native plants.
hp: Plant/animal disease vector. Vectors Pierce's disease (Xylella fastidiosa) to grapevines. On grape. Family Cicadellidae.

desc: Long-tailed mealybug — soft white waxy insect with very long waxy tail filaments (longer than body). Pest of citrus, grape, and ornamentals. Ants tend it for honeydew.

fm.Color: White waxy; tail filaments longer than body

hp: Sap feeder on citrus — honeydew producer.

desc: Barnacle scale — waxy scale insect resembling a small barnacle. On many ornamental and citrus plants. Produces honeydew attracting sooty mold.
hp: Barnacle wax scale — hemispherical scale on citrus, holly, and ornamentals. Waxy coating resembles barnacle. Honeydew promotes sooty mold.

desc: Mute stilt bug — extremely slender thread-like bug. Predator of mites and small insects on vegetation. So thin it's nearly invisible in profile.
hp: Generalist predator of insects and other arthropods. Family Berytidae.

desc: F-winged barklouse — small psocid on tree bark with distinctive F-shaped wing markings. Feeds on algae and lichens. Very common on tree trunks. Gregarious.

fm.Color: Pale with F-pattern wings; on tree bark

hp: Feeds on lichens and bark microflora. Family Stenopsocidae.

desc: Lupine aphid — large green aphid forming colonies on lupine stems and flower buds. Long cornicles. Can damage ornamental and native lupines.

fm.Color: Green; long black cornicles; on lupine

hp: Aphid — colonial sap-feeder on Lupinus (lupine) stems. Honeydew attracts ants.

desc: Catalina cicada — cicada associated with Santa Catalina Island and mainland coastal scrub. Males sing from shrubs. Part of California's diverse cicada fauna.
hp: Cicada hunter — catches cicadas in flight for larval provisions.

desc: Westphalian booklouse — tiny wingless psocid found indoors in damp areas. Feeds on mold and starchy materials. Can infest old books and papers.

fm.Color: Pale; wingless; in damp indoor areas

hp: Found in and around buildings and structures. Tiny. Feeds on mold and starchy materials.

desc: California plant bug — small green mirid on phacelia and other wildflowers. Native. Part of the enormous and diverse plant bug family.
hp: On flowers. Part of flower-visiting community. Preys on ant.

desc: Western big-eyed bug — tiny predatory bug with enormous eyes. Important biocontrol agent — feeds on aphids, whiteflies, mites, and insect eggs in crops.
hp: Biocontrol agent; aphid predator. Specialist aphid predator — important biocontrol. Preys on aphid. Tiny.

desc: Woolly whitefly — tiny moth-like insect producing dense woolly wax on citrus leaf undersides. Introduced from South America. Managed by parasitoid wasps.

fm.Color: White; woolly waxy tufts on citrus

hp: Sap feeder on Citrus. Produces honeydew promoting sooty mold. Agricultural pest. On Citrus (citrus). Managed by parasitoid wasps.

desc: Bronze bug — tiny flat bug from Australia damaging eucalyptus. Causes leaf bronzing and defoliation. Invasive pest spreading through California since ~2016.
hp: On eucalyptus — introduced with host tree. On Eucalyptus (eucalyptus). Defoliator.

desc: Sycamore seed bug — pale seed bug specializing on sycamore seeds. Can invade homes in large numbers in fall when sycamores drop seeds. Harmless but alarming in quantity.

fm.Behavior: Mass home invasions in fall

hp: On western sycamore (platanus racemosa). On Platanus (sycamore).

desc: Spiny assassin bug — dark assassin bug with spiny body surface. Predator of other insects. Found in leaf litter and under debris. Can deliver painful defensive bite.
hp: Predator in leaf litter — hunts small arthropods. Spiny/armed.

desc: Bluegum psyllid — tiny psyllid producing copious white waxy secretion on blue gum eucalyptus. Introduced from Australia. Can defoliate young trees.

fm.Color: Tiny; white wax on eucalyptus shoots

hp: On eucalyptus — introduced with host tree. On Eucalyptus (eucalyptus). Defoliator.

desc: Whitemarked fleahopper — tiny pale mirid. Major pest of cotton — damages developing squares. Also on many weedy plants. Jumps actively.
hp: Cotton pest — feeds on cotton bolls, leaves, or squares. Tiny.

desc: Stanford's whitefly — tiny whitefly on undersides of native tree leaves. Named for Stanford University collections. Produces waxy secretion.
hp: On undersides of native tree leaves. Part of the diverse native California whitefly community.

desc: Suckfly — small pale green plant bug feeding on tobacco, tomato, and other Solanaceae. Common garden pest. Causes stippling damage to leaves.
hp: Garden and crop pest. Herbivore on cultivated plants. On tomato.

desc: Red-winged seed bug — dark seed bug with reddish wing membrane. Under bark and in leaf litter. Feeds on seeds. Native.
hp: Sap-feeding hemipteran on host plant tissue. Family Rhyparochromidae.

desc: Tomato bug — small green-brown mirid found on tomato plants. Both pest (feeds on plant) and predator (eats whitefly eggs). Complex ecological role.
hp: Solanaceae associate — feeds on tomato, pepper, or nightshade family plants.

desc: Yerba santa plant bug — small green mirid associated with yerba santa (Eriodictyon) in chaparral. Host-specific. Part of diverse California native mirid fauna.
hp: Plant bug on Eriodictyon (yerba santa) in chaparral. Host-specific mirid on native medicinal shrub. Sap-feeder.

desc: Morrill lace bug — tiny bug with intricate lace-patterned wings on various shrubs. Causes stippling damage to leaves. Beautiful under magnification.
hp: On various native shrubs — causes stippling damage. Intricate lace-wing venation.

desc: Virgula plant bug — small predatory mirid feeding on mites, thrips, and small insects. Beneficial in gardens. Originally European.
hp: Generalist insect predator. Beneficial in gardens. Preys on thrips. Family Miridae.

desc: Minute pirate bug — tiny but fierce predator. Important biocontrol of thrips in strawberry and other crops. Can deliver surprisingly painful bite relative to tiny size.
hp: Minute pirate bug — tiny (2mm) but fierce predator of thrips, mites, and aphid eggs. One of the most important biocontrol agents in CA crops.

desc: Armored leafhopper — small brown leafhopper with pale crossband. Common on various shrubs in chaparral and gardens.
hp: Chaparral sap feeder — on native shrubs. Part of native plant-insect community. Family Cicadellidae. Armored.

desc: California barklouse — small psocid on tree bark. Feeds on algae, lichen, and fungi. Gregarious — often in groups. Harmless. Common on native oaks.
hp: Bark microflora feeder — on lichens, algae, and fungal spores on tree bark. On Quercus (oak).

desc: Acacia psyllid — tiny psyllid on Acacia and Albizia. Introduced from Australia. Forms dense colonies producing honeydew. Common in urban plantings.
hp: On Acacia and Albizia. Introduced from Australia with host plantings. Dense colony former.

desc: Asian woolly hackberry aphid — white woolly aphid introduced from Asia. Forms dense colonies on hackberry (Celtis) leaves. Produces copious honeydew.

fm.Color: White waxy coating on hackberry leaves

hp: Colonial sap feeder producing honeydew. Preys on aphid. Family Aphididae.

desc: Six-spotted leafhopper — small green leafhopper with six dark spots on vertex. On oak and other hardwoods. Named for entomologist McAtee.
hp: On coast live oak (quercus agrifolia). On Quercus (oak). Family Cicadellidae. Six-spotted.

desc: Cherubic gall aphid — aphid inducing swollen petiole galls on poplar leaves. Complex life cycle alternating between poplar (primary) and plant roots (secondary).
hp: Aphid predator. Important biocontrol agent. Specialist aphid predator — important biocontrol. Preys on aphid. Family Aphididae. Cherubic.

desc: Glaucous manzanita gall aphid — tiny aphid inducing red leaf-edge galls on manzanita. Related to T. coweni. Common in chaparral wherever manzanita grows.

fm.Color: Dark; red leaf-edge galls on manzanita

hp: On Arctostaphylos (manzanita) in chaparral. Native shrub specialist. Tiny. Tiny aphid inducing red leaf-edge galls on manzanita. Relate.

desc: Green citrus aphid — bright green aphid on citrus and spiraea. One of the most common aphid pests worldwide. Vectors citrus tristeza virus.
hp: Sap feeder on Citrus. Produces honeydew promoting sooty mold. Agricultural pest. On Citrus (citrus). Family Aphididae.

desc: Abbreviated stink bug — brown shield bug similar to Euschistus but smaller. On various plants. Less common than other stink bugs.
hp: On various plants — generalist sap feeder. Less common than Euschistus stink bugs.

desc: Blue-eyed plant bug — tiny mirid with distinctive blue-tinted eyes. On various native shrubs in chaparral. Attractive under magnification.
hp: Native chaparral scrub habitat. Preys on ant. Tiny.

desc: Red-collared seed bug — dark seed bug with reddish-orange collar behind head. Feeds on seeds of various plants. Common in weedy areas and under debris.
hp: Plant sap feeder on various hosts. Family Lygaeidae.

desc: Mediterranean mint aphid — small green aphid on mint family plants (Lamiaceae). Introduced from Mediterranean. Common on garden herbs.
hp: Sap-feeding hemipteran on host plant tissue. On Lamiaceae (mints).

desc: Western seed bug — small dark rhyparochromid found under debris and in leaf litter. Feeds on seeds. Common but secretive ground-dwelling species.
hp: Under debris on ground. In leaf litter. Family Rhyparochromidae.

desc: Obscure leafhopper — small pale leafhopper. Common but overlooked due to small size. On various grasses and herbs.
hp: On grasses and forbs in grassland. On Poaceae (grasses). Family Cicadellidae.

desc: Vachon's barklouse — small psocid on tree bark feeding on algae, fungi, and lichens. Gregarious — forms groups on bark surfaces. Harmless.
hp: Bark microflora feeder — on lichens, algae, and fungal spores on tree bark.

desc: Red-shouldered stink bug — green shield bug with distinctive reddish-orange shoulder stripe. On many plants including vegetables. Can damage tomatoes and berries.
hp: On many plants — generalist. Red-orange shoulder stripe distinguishes from other green stink bugs.

desc: Western red-shouldered stink bug — closely related to T. pallidovirens. Green with reddish shoulder band. On agricultural crops. Part of a difficult species complex.
hp: On vegetables, fruit, and native plants. Piercing-sucking damage to developing seeds and fruit.

desc: Potato psyllid — tiny psyllid vectoring zebra chip disease of potato (Candidatus Liberibacter). Major quarantine concern. Also on tomato and pepper.

fm.Warning: Vectors zebra chip disease — quarantine pest

hp: Potato/tomato psyllid — vectors zebra chip disease (Candidatus Liberibacter solanacearum) devastating potatoes. Major emerging crop pest.

desc: Ringed lace bug — tiny tingid with delicate wing venation. On various plants. Wing pattern resembles fine lacework — beautiful under magnification.
hp: On various shrubs — leaf underside feeder causing stippling. Delicate lace-patterned wings.

desc: Oak wax scale — small scale insect producing distinctive waxy secretion on oak bark. Native. Part of the diverse sap-feeding insect community on California oaks.
hp: On oak bark. Part of bark-dwelling arthropod community. On Quercus (oak). Family Cerococcidae.

desc: Spotted-winged psyllid — tiny psyllid with dark wing spots. On various plants. Part of California's diverse psyllid fauna.
hp: On various plants — spotted-winged psyllid. Part of California's diverse native psyllid fauna.

desc: Woolly-legged assassin bug — slender thread-legged bug with hairy legs. Remarkable — enters spider webs to steal prey or eat the spider itself. Kleptoparasite.

fm.Behavior: Enters spider webs — steals prey or eats spider

hp: Sap-feeding hemipteran on host plant tissue. Family Reduviidae.

desc: Island mallow psyllid — tiny psyllid on tree mallow (Malva). Introduced from Mediterranean. Can damage ornamental mallows. Waxy secretion on leaves.
hp: Specialist on tree mallow (Malva). Introduced from Mediterranean. Can damage ornamental plantings.

desc: Van Duzee's treehopper — small brown treehopper with elongated pronotal horn. On oak twigs. Part of diverse treehopper community on California oaks.

fm.Color: Brown; pronotal horn; on oak twigs

hp: On coast live oak (quercus agrifolia). On Quercus (oak). Elongated.

desc: Thread bug — extremely slender assassin bug. Predatory on small insects in spider webs and bark crevices. So thin it's nearly invisible in profile.
hp: Builds web to capture insects and spiders.

desc: Ice plant scale — soft scale heavily infesting ice plant (Carpobrotus). Produces white cottony egg sacs. Can damage ornamental and invasive ice plant groundcover.

fm.Color: Brown with white cottony egg sac; on ice plant

hp: Sessile sap feeder — honeydew producer. On cotton. Family Coccidae.

desc: Wright's seed bug — small dark lygaeid found in leaf litter and under debris. Seed-feeding. Named for entomologist Wright. Part of diverse ground-dwelling bug community.
hp: Under debris on ground. In leaf litter. Family Lygaeidae.

desc: Arizona thread-legged bug — extremely slender stick-like assassin bug. Thread-thin legs and body. Predator in vegetation. Among the most bizarre-looking LA County insects.
hp: Generalist predator of insects and other arthropods. Family Reduviidae.

desc: Nigra scale — dark soft scale on ornamental plants and citrus. Produces honeydew attracting sooty mold. Managed by parasitoid wasps and ladybugs.
hp: Nigra scale — black hemispherical scale on citrus and ornamentals. Managed by parasitoid wasps (Coccophagus, Metaphycus). Honeydew producer.

desc: Hemispherical scale — smooth dark dome-shaped soft scale on many ornamental plants. Produces honeydew. Named for occurrence on coffee plants. Pantropical.
hp: Builds horizontal sheet/dome web. Family Coccidae.

desc: Delicate planthopper — tiny delphacid planthopper on grasses and sedges. One of many small planthoppers in grassland and marsh habitats.
hp: On grasses and forbs in grassland. On Poaceae (grasses). Marsh/wetland.

desc: Eucalyptus shoot psyllid — tiny psyllid on eucalyptus growing tips. Introduced from Australia. Produces white lerp (waxy cover). Damages new growth.
hp: On eucalyptus — introduced with host tree. On Eucalyptus (eucalyptus). Tiny. Tiny psyllid on eucalyptus growing tips. Introduced from Aus.

desc: Bristly psyllid — small psyllid on various native shrubs. Part of the diverse native psyllid fauna of California chaparral.
hp: Native chaparral scrub habitat. Family Psyllidae.

desc: Curtain fig psyllid — tiny psyllid on Ficus species. Introduced from Asia. Causes leaf curling and sooty mold on ornamental figs.
hp: Specialist on Ficus species. Introduced from Asia. Causes severe leaf curling and sooty mold.

desc: Van Duzee's plant bug — small green mirid on various plants. Named for pioneer West Coast hemipterist E.P. Van Duzee.
hp: On various plants. Omnivorous — sap and small arthropods. Part of native mirid community.

desc: Elder aphid — colonial aphid forming dense clusters on elderberry (Sambucus) shoots. Dark green to black. Tended by ants for honeydew. Spring peaks.
hp: Aphid on Sambucus (elderberry) — colonial sap-feeder on shoots and flower clusters. Honeydew attracts ants. Native host-plant association.

desc: Pine needle aphid — elongated gray-green aphid feeding singly (not colonial) on pine needles. Unusual among aphids in being solitary. Waxy coating.

fm.Color: Gray-green with waxy bloom; solitary on pine needles

hp: Aphid — colonial sap feeder producing honeydew. Ant-tended.

desc: Olive psyllid — tiny psyllid on olive trees producing white waxy secretions on shoots. Can reduce fruit set. Introduced from Mediterranean. Pest of ornamental and production olives.

fm.Color: Green; white wax on olive shoots

hp: Olive (Olea europaea) pest — larvae in olive fruit. On olive. Tiny.

desc: Lesser lawn leafhopper — tiny green leafhopper on turf grass. Can transmit plant diseases. Very common in irrigated lawns.

fm.Color: Pale green; tiny; on lawn grass

hp: Plant disease vector — transmits pathogens via piercing-sucking feeding. Preys on ant. Tiny.

desc: Strauch's barklouse — small psocid on tree bark. Feeds on microflora (algae, fungi). Gregarious. One of several common bark-dwelling psocids in LA.
hp: Mycetophagous — feeds on fungi. Fungal associate — see lafungi.org. Family Ectopsocidae.

desc: Cowpea aphid — shiny black aphid on legumes (cowpea, alfalfa, beans). Vector of multiple plant viruses. Forms dense colonies on stems and pods.
hp: Aphid predator. Important biocontrol agent. Specialist aphid predator — important biocontrol. On alfalfa. Family Aphididae.

desc: Hedge nettle stink bug — dark stink bug with orange-red shoulder markings. On hedge nettle (Stachys) and other Lamiaceae. Native. Attractive pattern.
hp: Specialist on hedge nettle (Stachys). Native host-specific stink bug on a native plant.

desc: Ringed-legged assassin bug — dark assassin bug with distinctively banded legs. Predatory on other insects in leaf litter and under bark.
hp: Predator in leaf litter — hunts small arthropods. Family Reduviidae.

desc: Notable water strider — large water strider skating on pond and stream surfaces. Larger than common Gerris. Detects prey vibrations on water film.

fm.Color: Dark brown; skates on water surface

hp: Stream and river habitats. Aquatic or semi-aquatic lifestyle.

desc: Western plant bug — small predatory mirid. Important natural enemy of whiteflies and spider mites on cotton and vegetables. Beneficial.
hp: Cotton pest — feeds on cotton bolls, leaves, or squares.

desc: Horn's shore bug — tiny semi-aquatic bug of stream margins and wet rocks. Predatory on small arthropods. One of the smallest predatory true bugs.

fm.Color: Dark; on wet rocks near water

hp: Predatory hemipteran — captures other insects with raptorial forelegs. Smallest.

desc: Beeblossom aphid — green-pink aphid specific to beeblossom (Gaura/Oenothera). Host-specific. Part of the large Macrosiphum genus of plant-specific aphids.
hp: Aphid predator. Important biocontrol agent. Specialist aphid predator — important biocontrol. Preys on aphid.

desc: Reduced leafhopper — small greenish leafhopper on various shrubs. Part of the diverse leafhopper fauna of California chaparral.
hp: Chaparral leafhopper — on native shrubs in coastal sage scrub and chaparral. Part of the native plant-insect community. Sap feeder.

desc: Smoky poplar aphid — dark aphid on poplar and cottonwood leaves. Colonial. Tends to be tended by ants for honeydew.
hp: On cottonwood/poplar (populus) in riparian habitat. On Populus (cottonwood). Family Aphididae.

desc: Chenopodia plant bug — tiny mirid associated with goosefoot family plants. Recent arrival to California. On Chenopodium and related weeds.
hp: On goosefoot family plants (Chenopodiaceae). Recent California colonist. Sap feeder.

desc: Sand bug — small flat dark seed bug living in sandy soil. Feeds on fallen seeds. Found under debris in dry sandy habitats. Camouflaged against sand.
hp: Cryptically camouflaged against habitat substrate. Soil-dwelling.

desc: Reflex scentless plant bug — small green rhopalid. On various weedy plants. Part of the scentless plant bug family — lacks the stink glands of true stink bugs.
hp: On various weedy plants. Scentless — lacks defensive stink glands unlike true stink bugs.

desc: Oblique shield bug — small scutellerid with shield covering entire abdomen. On various plants. Metallic-tinted. Part of the shield bug family.
hp: Builds horizontal sheet/dome web. Metallic.

desc: Hooked plant bug — small mirid with hooked clasper structures in males. On various plants in chaparral and gardens.
hp: Sap-feeding hemipteran on host plant tissue. Preys on ant. Chaparral. Hooked.

desc: Red-veined mirid — green plant bug with reddish wing veins. On various plants. Can damage cotton and vegetable crops.
hp: On various crops — can damage cotton and vegetable buds. Red wing veins diagnostic.

desc: Bead-like cottonwood gall aphid — induces distinctive bead-shaped galls on cottonwood leaf petioles. Complex life cycle alternating between cottonwood and roots of other plants.

fm.Color: In bead-shaped galls on cottonwood petioles

hp: On cottonwood/poplar (populus) in riparian habitat. On Populus (cottonwood). Family Aphididae.

desc: Wounded flatid — small green planthopper. Wedge-shaped at rest. On various ornamental and wild plants. Produces white waxy secretion.
hp: Sap feeder on various plants. Wedge-shaped planthopper producing waxy secretions on foliage.

desc: Broad-headed cicada — small cicada with proportionally wide head. Males produce clicking (not buzzing) song. In chaparral and scrub. Hard to locate by sound.
hp: Native chaparral scrub habitat. Root-feeding nymph underground. Family Cicadidae. Broad-headed.

desc: Minute psyllid — tiny psyllid on various plants. Part of the diverse Bactericera genus which includes several important crop psyllids.
hp: On various plants. Tiny sap-feeding psyllid. Part of the large Bactericera genus.

desc: Majestic leafhopper — medium brown leafhopper with pale markings on forewings. On various trees and shrubs in woodland and chaparral. Can vector aster yellows phytoplasma to various plants. Part of the diverse Scaphytopius — a large New World leafhopper genus.
hp: On Asteraceae (composites) — daisies, sunflowers, sagebrush.

desc: Marbled shield bug — small scutellerid with marbled pattern. Shield covers entire abdomen and wings. On various plants. Attractive patterning.
hp: Builds horizontal sheet/dome web. Family Scutelleridae.

desc: Prickly pear cochineal — scale insect on Opuntia cactus. Produces carmine red dye. White cottony clusters on cactus pads. Used commercially for natural red dye for centuries.

fm.Color: White cottony masses on cactus; red when crushed
fm.Note: Source of natural carmine red dye

hp: Specialist on cactus (cactus spp.). On Opuntia (cactus). Family Dactylopiidae.

desc: Tiger scentless plant bug — small rhopalid with tiger-like striped pattern. On various plants. Mediterranean origin. Expanding in SoCal.

fm.Color: Brown with dark stripes — tiger pattern

hp: On various plants. Mediterranean introduction. Scentless plant bug with tiger-striped pattern.

desc: Corymbia psyllid — tiny psyllid on Corymbia (formerly Eucalyptus) species. Introduced from Australia. Part of the guild of eucalyptus-associated psyllids in LA.
hp: On eucalyptus — introduced with host tree. On Eucalyptus (eucalyptus). Tiny.

desc: Black plant bug — small dark mirid. On various native and ornamental plants. Part of the diverse mirid fauna of California.
hp: On native and ornamental plants. Sap feeder. Part of the diverse native California mirid fauna.

desc: Mexican leafhopper — small elongated leafhopper on grasses. Very common in grassland, turf, and agricultural areas. Named for Swedish entomologist Jacob Spångberg. Feeds on grass sap. Part of the dominant grass-associated leafhopper community throughout SoCal.
hp: Grass sap-feeder (Poaceae). Part of grassland herbivore community. On Poaceae (grasses). Elongated.

desc: Tuliptree scale — large conspicuous soft scale on tulip tree, magnolia, and linden. Up to ½ inch — one of the largest scale insects. Produces copious honeydew.
hp: Predator of scale insects — biocontrol in orchards and gardens. Preys on scale insect.

desc: Unadorned cactus bug — leaf-footed bug on prickly pear cactus fruit. Plain brown without the ornate tibial expansions of some coreids. Feeds on cactus juice.
hp: Specialist on cactus (cactus spp.). On Opuntia (cactus). Family Coreidae.

desc: Short psyllid — tiny psyllid on various shrubs. Part of the diverse Cacopsylla genus which includes many plant-specific species.
hp: On various shrubs. Tiny jumping plant louse. Part of the diverse native psyllid community.

desc: Hollyleaf cherry whitefly — tiny whitefly specific to hollyleaf cherry (Prunus ilicifolia). Native. Part of the specialized insect fauna on this California native shrub.
hp: Sap-feeding hemipteran on host plant tissue. On Prunus (cherry).

desc: Gray lawn leafhopper — tiny gray leafhopper very common in turf grass. Can vector phytoplasmas. Often extremely abundant in lawns.

fm.Color: Gray; very tiny; in lawn grass

hp: Plant disease vector — transmits pathogens via piercing-sucking feeding. Preys on ant. Tiny.

desc: Scaled leafhopper — small brown leafhopper on oaks and other trees. Part of the diverse canopy leafhopper community.
hp: On Quercus agrifolia (coast live oak). Sap feeder — ant-tended.

desc: Turnip aphid — gray-green aphid on brassica crops. Dense colonies on turnip, radish, and mustard. Important pest. Waxy coating gives dusty appearance.

fm.Color: Gray-green with waxy bloom; on brassicas

hp: On brassicaceae (mustard family). Agricultural pest. Preys on aphid.

desc: Van Duzee's cicada — small clicking cicada of oak woodland. Males produce series of clicks rather than continuous buzz. Named for hemipterist Van Duzee.
hp: On coast live oak (quercus agrifolia). Associated with Coast Live Oak. On Quercus (oak). Woodland. Van.

desc: Beautiful-winged psocid — small barklouse with attractively patterned wings. On tree bark feeding on algae and lichen. Gregarious.
hp: Feeds on lichens and bark microflora. Family Psocidae.

desc: Tamarix leafhopper — small leafhopper introduced with salt cedar (Tamarix). Host-specific — only on tamarix. May serve as inadvertent biocontrol of invasive salt cedar.
hp: Introduced for biological pest control. Family Cicadellidae.

desc: Rusty plum aphid — reddish-brown aphid alternating between plum (winter) and grasses (summer). Complex life cycle. Can damage plum tree leaves.
hp: On grasses and forbs in grassland. On Poaceae (grasses). Family Aphididae.

desc: Giant reed aphid — large dark aphid on giant reed (Arundo donax). One of the larger aphid species. Host-specific on Arundo. Dense colonies on stems.

fm.Color: Dark brown; on giant reed stems

hp: Aphid predator. Important biocontrol agent. Specialist aphid predator — important biocontrol. Preys on aphid.

desc: Melaleuca psyllid — tiny psyllid on melaleuca/paperbark trees. Introduced from Australia with its host tree. Produces white waxy secretion.
hp: On tree bark. Part of bark-dwelling community. Tiny.

desc: Cotton lace bug — tiny bug with intricate lace-like wing pattern. On cotton, hibiscus, and other mallows. Feeding causes pale stippling on leaf upper surface.
hp: Plant sap feeder on various hosts. On cotton.

desc: Currant psyllid — tiny psyllid on Ribes (currant and gooseberry). Can cause leaf curling and distortion on host plants. Native species on native hosts.
hp: Specialist on Ribes (currant, gooseberry). Causes leaf curling. Native bug on native shrubs.

desc: Essig's woolly oak aphid — white woolly aphid on coast live oak undersides. Named for pioneering CA entomologist E.O. Essig. Produces white waxy wool.

fm.Color: White woolly; on oak leaf undersides

hp: Sap feeder on oaks. Associated with Coast Live Oak. On Quercus (oak). Family Aphididae.

desc: Plum-thistle aphid — green-brown aphid alternating between plum trees (winter host) and thistles (summer host). Classic host-alternating life cycle. Can damage both hosts.
hp: Aphid predator. Important biocontrol agent. Specialist aphid predator — important biocontrol. Preys on aphid. Family Aphididae.

desc: California dictyopharid — small elongated planthopper with pointed head. On grasses in coastal and foothill habitats. Distinctive long narrow profile.
hp: On grasses and forbs in grassland. On Poaceae (grasses).

desc: Shiny seed bug — small dark shiny seed bug under debris. Feeds on fallen seeds. Common but secretive. Ground-dwelling.
hp: Ground-dwelling seed feeder — on fallen seeds under litter. Seed predation affects plant regeneration. Feeds on fallen seeds.

desc: Chinese wax scale — soft scale insect producing thick waxy coating on plant stems. Produces copious honeydew leading to sooty mold. Pest of citrus and ornamentals.
hp: Sap feeder on Citrus. Produces honeydew promoting sooty mold. Agricultural pest. On Citrus (citrus). Family Coccidae.

desc: Sycamore scale — tiny scale insect specific to sycamore trees. Lives in bark crevices. Can cause branch dieback in heavy infestations. Native.
hp: On western sycamore (platanus racemosa). On Platanus (sycamore). Tiny.

desc: Giant willow aphid — one of the largest aphids in the world (up to 6mm). Dark brown with prominent dorsal tubercle. Colonial on willow bark. Impressive for an aphid.

fm.Color: Dark brown; prominent back bump; on willow bark

hp: On willows (salix) in riparian habitat. On Salix (willow). Large (6mm).

desc: Bird cherry-oat aphid — green aphid alternating between bird cherry (winter) and cereals (summer). Important vector of barley yellow dwarf virus worldwide.
