hp: Aphid predator. Important biocontrol agent. Specialist aphid predator — important biocontrol. On Prunus (cherry).

desc: Onion aphid — pale green aphid specializing on onion family plants (Allium). Can damage garlic, leeks, and ornamental alliums. Dense colonies on leaves.
hp: Aphid predator. Important biocontrol agent. Specialist aphid predator — important biocontrol. Preys on aphid. Family Aphididae. Onion.

desc: Western dicyphus — small green-black predatory mirid. Important biocontrol agent — feeds on whiteflies, spider mites, and moth eggs in greenhouses.
hp: Biocontrol agent; spider predator. Preys on whiteflies. Biocontrol agent. Family Miridae.

desc: Coquillett's plant bug — small green mirid. Named for pioneering dipterist D.W. Coquillett. On various native plants in chaparral.
hp: Native chaparral scrub habitat. Preys on ant. Family Miridae.

desc: Smooth seed bug — small dark lygaeid found under debris. Feeds on seeds. Secretive ground-dweller of dry habitats.
hp: Ground-dwelling seed feeder — on fallen seeds under litter. Seed predation affects plant regeneration. Family Lygaeidae.

desc: Beet leafhopper — tiny pale leafhopper. Major vector of curly top virus devastating beets, tomatoes, and many crops. One of the most important pest leafhoppers in western agriculture.

fm.Disease: Vectors curly top virus — major crop disease

hp: Plant/animal disease vector. Agricultural/garden pest. On tomato.

desc: Ficus whitefly — tiny whitefly specific to ficus trees. Introduced from Asia. Can cause severe defoliation of ornamental ficus. Very common in urban LA.
hp: Specialist on ficus trees. Introduced from Asia. Can cause severe defoliation of ornamental figs.

desc: Fig leaf-rolling psyllid — tiny psyllid causing distinctive leaf-rolling on ficus. Can create dense populations on ornamental figs. Introduced.
hp: Specialist on ficus — causes distinctive leaf-rolling. Dense infestations on figs.

desc: Confused pirate bug — tiny predatory anthocorid feeding on thrips in leaf curl galls. Introduced for biocontrol. Important natural enemy of pest thrips.
hp: Thrips predator — tiny anthocorid specializing on thrips in curled leaves and flower galls. Biocontrol of Cuban laurel thrips on ficus.

desc: Bald planthopper — small cixiid planthopper. Larvae feed on plant roots underground. Adults hop among vegetation. Part of the cryptic root-feeding insect community.
hp: Sap-feeding hemipteran on host plant tissue. Preys on ant. Cryptic.

desc: Bay sucker — tiny psyllid forming pit galls on bay laurel (Laurus) leaves. Introduced from Europe. Common on ornamental bay laurel in LA gardens.

fm.Color: Tiny; pit galls on bay laurel leaves

hp: Plant gall-inducer — larvae develop inside species-specific gall tissue.

desc: Manzanita cicada — medium dark cicada singing from manzanita and other chaparral shrubs. Opaque wings. Buzzing song in summer heat. Foothill species.

fm.Color: Dark; opaque wings; sings from manzanita

hp: On Arctostaphylos (manzanita) in chaparral. Native shrub specialist. Family Cicadidae.

desc: Robust ceanothus bug — green mirid plant bug associated with ceanothus (California lilac). Host-specific native species.
hp: On ceanothus (california lilac) — native shrub specialist. On Ceanothus (ceanothus). Robust.

desc: Black stink bug — small dark stink bug with punctured surface. On various plants. Can emit defensive stink. Less common than green stink bugs.
hp: On various plants — generalist sap feeder. Dark stink bug with punctured surface texture.

desc: Rose gum psyllid — tiny psyllid on Eucalyptus grandis (rose gum). Introduced from Australia with its host tree. Can cause severe leaf distortion.
hp: On Eucalyptus — introduced with host tree from Australia.

desc: Kirby's backswimmer — aquatic bug swimming upside-down. Predatory — catches aquatic insects and tadpoles. Can deliver painful bite. In ponds and pools.
hp: Predatory hemipteran — captures other insects with raptorial forelegs. Family Notonectidae.

desc: Juniper stink bug — green shield bug on juniper and other conifers. Well-camouflaged on green foliage. Can produce defensive odor when handled.
hp: Cryptically camouflaged against habitat substrate. Family Pentatomidae.

desc: Blood-colored milkweed bug — red-orange and black seed bug on milkweed. Sequesters cardiac glycosides from milkweed seeds making it distasteful to predators.
hp: On Asclepias (milkweed). Aposematic — sequesters cardiac glycosides. Red-orange and black seed bug on milkweed. Sequesters cardia.

desc: Nettle ground bug — small brown bug on nettles and related plants. Introduced from Europe. Common in moist disturbed habitats.
hp: Specialist on nettles (Urtica). Introduced from Europe. Common in weedy urban areas.

desc: Mealy willow aphid — produces conspicuous white waxy powder on willow shoots. Colonial. Important food source for ladybug larvae and other predators.
hp: Aphid — colonial sap feeder on willow. Produces honeydew attracting ants.

desc: Hibiscus scentless plant bug — pale rhopalid associated with hibiscus and related mallows. Feeds on developing seeds. Common in ornamental plantings.

fm.Color: Pale brown; on hibiscus seed pods

hp: Plant sap feeder on various hosts. On Malvaceae (mallows).

desc: Bear assassin bug — dark robust assassin bug. Predatory ambush hunter in leaf litter. Can deliver painful defensive bite.
hp: Predatory hemipteran — captures other insects with raptorial forelegs. Robust.

desc: Tobira psyllid — tiny psyllid specific to Pittosporum tobira. Can cause leaf distortion and honeydew problems on ornamental tobira hedges. Introduced from East Asia.
hp: Specialist on Pittosporum tobira. Can cause leaf distortion and honeydew on ornamental hedges.

desc: One-spotted leafhopper — tiny pale leafhopper with single dark spot. On various plants. Part of the enormous and species-rich leafhopper family (40,000+ species).
hp: On various plants. Tiny leafhopper — part of the enormous canopy leafhopper community.

desc: Avocado lace bug — tiny lace-winged bug on avocado leaf undersides causing brown stippling. Introduced from tropical Americas. Pest of backyard avocados.
hp: Avocado (Persea americana) associate.

desc: Volxem's coreid — medium brown leaf-footed bug. Introduced from Mediterranean. On various plants. Expanding in SoCal.
hp: On various plants. Introduced from Mediterranean. Expanded hind tibiae (leaf-footed bug).

desc: Human louse — ectoparasitic insect living on human body/clothing. Blood-feeder. Can vector typhus and relapsing fever. Medically significant.
hp: Human louse — obligate ectoparasite. Body louse vectors typhus and relapsing fever. Head louse does not transmit disease. Ancient human associate.

desc: Bordering barklouse — small psocid on tree bark feeding on algae, lichens, and fungal growth. Gregarious — forms herds on bark. Harmless decomposer contributing to nutrient cycling on tree surfaces. The gregarious bark-dwelling habit makes them easy to observe.
hp: Detritivore — breaks down dead organic matter. Important in nutrient cycling. Family Psocidae.

desc: Speckled barklouse — small psocid on tree bark. Feeds on microflora. One of many bark-dwelling psocids that form the overlooked bark micro-ecosystem.
hp: On tree bark. Part of bark-dwelling community. Family Psocidae.

desc: Two-lined burrowing bug — small dark burrower bug living in soil around plant roots. Shiny. Two pale lines on pronotum. Feeds on root sap.

fm.Color: Dark shiny; two pale pronotal lines; in soil

hp: Plant sap feeder on various hosts. Preys on ant. Soil-dwelling.

desc: Oblique-banded seed bug — small dark seed bug with oblique pale wing band. Under debris feeding on seeds. Common in weedy areas.

fm.Color: Dark with oblique pale wing band

hp: Plant sap feeder on various hosts. Family Rhyparochromidae.

desc: Narrow scentless plant bug — slender pale rhopalid on various plants. Unlike stink bugs, lacks strong defensive odor. On weedy and native plants.
hp: On various plants. Scentless — lacks defensive stink glands. Slender rhopalid.

desc: Small shield bug — tiny scutellerid with shield covering entire abdomen. On grasses. One of the smallest shield bugs. Easily overlooked.

fm.Color: Brown; shield covers entire back; very small

hp: On grasses and forbs in grassland. On Poaceae (grasses). Tiny.

desc: Corn leaf aphid — blue-green aphid on corn and other grasses. Important vector of barley yellow dwarf virus. Can reduce corn yields when severe.
hp: On grasses and forbs in grassland. On corn.

desc: Long-tailed eucalyptus psyllid — tiny psyllid with elongated anal tube. On eucalyptus. Introduced from Australia. Can cause leaf deformation.
hp: On eucalyptus — introduced with host tree. On Eucalyptus (eucalyptus). Elongated.

desc: California lilac aphid — aphid specific to ceanothus (California lilac). Native species on a native host plant. Can form large colonies in spring.
hp: On ceanothus (california lilac) — native shrub specialist. On Ceanothus (ceanothus).

desc: Green grass leafhopper — bright green leafhopper on grasses and sedges. Jumps powerfully when disturbed. Common but overlooked in lawn and grassland habitats.
hp: Leafhopper — xylem/phloem sap feeder. Part of canopy insect community. On Poaceae (grasses).

desc: Impressed-neck seed bug — small dark seed bug with pitted pronotum. Under debris in dry habitats. Feeds on fallen seeds.
hp: Plant sap feeder on various hosts. Family Lygaeidae.

desc: White-marked plant bug — small green-white mirid on various plants. Feeds on plant sap but also predatory on small insects. Beneficial in some crops.
hp: Generalist predator of insects and other arthropods. Preys on ant. Family Miridae.

desc: Crapemyrtle aphid — tiny yellow aphid specific to crapemyrtle trees. Produces copious honeydew causing sooty mold. Introduced from Asia. Very common.

fm.Color: Yellow; on crapemyrtle; causes sooty mold

hp: Colonial sap feeder producing honeydew. Preys on aphid. Tiny.

desc: White psyllid — pale tiny psyllid on native shrubs. Part of the diverse Cacopsylla genus with many host-specific species.
hp: On native shrubs. Part of the diverse Cacopsylla genus — many host-specific California species.

desc: Mistletoe psyllid — tiny psyllid forming galls on mistletoe (Phoradendron). Host-specific. Part of the diverse psyllid fauna associated with California native plants.
hp: Specialist on mistletoe (Phoradendron). Forms galls on parasitic mistletoe — a parasite of a parasite.

desc: Pasadena treehopper — small brown treehopper named for Pasadena, CA. On oak and other trees. Horn-shaped pronotum. Part of diverse treehopper fauna.

fm.Color: Brown; horn-shaped pronotum; named for Pasadena

hp: On coast live oak (quercus agrifolia). On Quercus (oak). Family Membracidae.

desc: Ramona plant bug — small green mirid on native vegetation in chaparral. Named for Ramona, CA (type locality in San Diego County). On various chaparral shrubs. Part of the California-centric mirid fauna — a significant proportion of our plant bug species are regional endemics.
hp: Native chaparral scrub habitat. Preys on ant. Family Miridae. Small green mirid on native vegetation in chaparral. Named f.

desc: Somber cicada — dark cicada of foothill chaparral. Males produce buzzing song from shrubs. Less common than other Okanagana species.
hp: Native chaparral scrub habitat. Root-feeding nymph underground. Family Cicadidae. Somber.

desc: Ragged leafhopper — small brown leafhopper with worn, ragged-looking wing markings (pannosa = ragged/tattered). On various shrubs in chaparral. Part of the diverse leafhopper fauna of California native plants — each shrub species supports its own leafhopper community.
hp: Ragged leafhopper — on chaparral shrubs. Brown with worn-looking markings (pannosa = ragged). Part of native chaparral insect community.

desc: Handsome cicada — medium cicada with clear wings. Males sing loudly from shrubs in chaparral. As the name suggests, one of the more attractive cicadas.
hp: Native chaparral scrub habitat. Root-feeding nymph underground. Family Cicadidae. Handsome.

desc: Rose-colored plant bug — small reddish mirid on various plants. Feeds on plant sap. Part of the enormous and species-rich plant bug genus Phytocoris.
hp: On Rosa (roses) — garden and wild species. On Rosa (rose). Enormous.

desc: Short-stigma psyllid — tiny psyllid on native shrubs in chaparral. Part of the diverse California native psyllid fauna.
hp: Native chaparral scrub habitat. Tiny.

desc: Black-green plant bug — small dark mirid with greenish tinge. On native plants. Part of the diverse mirid fauna of California.
hp: On native plants. Part of the diverse mirid community on California native shrubs.

desc: Red-veined cicada — cicada with distinctive reddish wing veins. Males sing from shrubs in foothill habitats. Part of California's endemic cicada fauna.
hp: Root-feeding nymph underground for years. Adult male sings from foothill shrubs. Red wing veins.

desc: Brown stink bug — common brown shield bug with defensive stink glands. On many plants including vegetables and row crops. Can damage developing fruit.
hp: On many plants including vegetables. Major crop pest — piercing-sucking damage to fruit and seeds.

desc: Potato leafhopper — tiny green wedge-shaped leafhopper. Major pest causing 'hopperburn' on potatoes, beans, and alfalfa. Migrant from eastern US.
hp: Alfalfa associate — larvae or adults feed on alfalfa (Medicago sativa). On potato.

desc: Modest plant bug — small green mirid on various plants. Part of the diverse but poorly known native plant bug fauna.
hp: On various native plants. Part of the poorly known native California mirid fauna.

desc: Southern small milkweed bug — red-black seed bug feeding on milkweed seeds. Smaller than large milkweed bug. Sequesters toxins. Gregarious.
hp: On milkweed — sequesters cardiac glycosides. On Asclepias (milkweed).

desc: Minor stink bug — small pale elongated stink bug on grasses. More slender than typical pentatomids. Feeds on grass seeds.

fm.Color: Pale; elongated for a stink bug; on grasses

hp: Grass sap-feeder (Poaceae). Part of grassland herbivore community. On Poaceae (grasses). Elongated.

desc: Coquillett's seed bug — small dark seed bug under debris. Named for pioneering dipterist D.W. Coquillett. Ground-dwelling.
hp: Ground-dwelling seed feeder — on fallen seeds under litter. Seed predation affects plant regeneration. Coquillett's.

desc: Vertical plant bug — small green mirid. Both herbivorous (sap-feeding) and predatory on mites and small insects. On various shrubs and herbs.
hp: Generalist predator of insects and other arthropods. Preys on ant. Family Miridae.

desc: California laurel aphid — woolly aphid producing white waxy tufts on California bay laurel (Umbellularia) leaves. Native species on a native tree.

fm.Color: White woolly tufts on bay laurel

hp: Aphid predator. Important biocontrol agent. Specialist aphid predator — important biocontrol. Preys on aphid. On California bay.

desc: Rose grain aphid — green aphid alternating between roses (winter) and cereals (summer). Important crop pest. Can transmit barley yellow dwarf virus.
hp: Rose-grain aphid — alternates between Rosa (winter) and cereal crops (summer). Heteroecious.

desc: Ash whitefly — tiny whitefly on ash, pomegranate, and other trees. Introduced from Mediterranean. Managed effectively by introduced parasitoid Encarsia inaron.
hp: Ash whitefly — on ash, pomegranate, citrus. Managed by introduced parasitoid Encarsia inaron — a classic biological control success story.

desc: Prickly lettuce aphid — pink-green aphid on lettuce and related Asteraceae. Can damage lettuce crops. Produces honeydew.
hp: Colonial sap feeder producing honeydew. On lettuce.

desc: Tipped plant bug — small dark mirid with pale-tipped cuneus. Predatory — feeds on mites, thrips, and small soft-bodied insects. Beneficial in gardens. The dark body with pale wing tip is diagnostic. Active on foliage and flowers. Native.
hp: Generalist insect predator. Beneficial in gardens. Preys on thrips. Family Miridae.

desc: Elegant plant bug — small mirid with attractively marked reddish-brown and pale pattern. On native vegetation in chaparral and sage scrub. Feeds on plant sap. 'Elegans' reflects the unusually refined markings — many mirids are drab green or brown.
hp: Coastal sage scrub and chaparral. Associated with Black Sage. Preys on ant. Family Miridae.

desc: Mountain mahogany psyllid — tiny psyllid specific to mountain mahogany (Cercocarpus). Native species on a native host. Chaparral habitat.
hp: Native chaparral scrub habitat. Montane.

desc: Blackberry leafhopper — small green-yellow leafhopper on blackberry and raspberry. Can cause white stippling on leaves. Common in gardens.
hp: Leafhopper — xylem/phloem sap feeder. Part of canopy insect community.

desc: Pale desert plant bug — tiny pale mirid of desert and arid scrub. On various plants including saltbush and sage.
hp: In coastal sage scrub habitat. Associated with Black Sage. Preys on ant.

desc: Eroded leafhopper — small pale leafhopper on various trees. Part of the enormous and diverse canopy leafhopper community. The genus Zyginama is a large western North American radiation with many undescribed species, each associated with particular woody plant genera.
hp: On various trees. Part of the enormous canopy leafhopper community — diverse Zyginama radiation.

desc: California oak aphid — tiny aphid on coast live oak (Q. agrifolia) leaves. Very common — almost every live oak has them. Native.

fm.Color: Pale; on oak leaf undersides; very common

hp: Sap feeder on oaks. Associated with Coast Live Oak. On Quercus (oak). Tiny.

desc: Spiny leafhopper — small brown leafhopper with distinctively spiny vertex (top of head). On oaks and other trees in woodland habitats.
hp: On coast live oak (quercus agrifolia). On Quercus (oak). Woodland.

desc: Extended seed bug — small pale seed bug with extended body proportions. Feeds on seeds of various plants in dry habitats. Under debris and leaf litter. Part of the ground-dwelling seed bug guild — an important community of seed predators influencing plant regeneration.
hp: Ground-dwelling seed feeder — on fallen seeds under litter. Seed predation affects plant regeneration. Preys on ant.

desc: Chenopod psyllid — tiny psyllid on goosefoot family plants (Chenopodium). Forms galls on leaves. On weedy plants.
hp: Specialist on goosefoot (Chenopodium). Induces leaf galls. On native and weedy chenopods.

desc: Reticulate-winged booklouse — tiny wingless psocid in damp indoor spaces. Feeds on mold and starchy materials. Can infest stored foods and old books.
hp: Reticulate booklouse — indoor psocid feeding on mold and starchy materials in damp conditions. Indicator of excess humidity. Harmless.

desc: Kellogg's leafhopper — small leafhopper on oaks. Named for entomologist Vernon Kellogg. Part of the oak canopy insect community.
hp: On coast live oak (quercus agrifolia). On Quercus (oak). Family Cicadellidae. On Kellogg.

desc: Purple scale — elongated curved armored scale on citrus. Major citrus pest. Mussel-shaped cover distinguishes from round California red scale. Biocontrol by parasitoids.
hp: Sap feeder on Citrus. Produces honeydew promoting sooty mold. Agricultural pest. On Citrus (citrus). Parasitoid. Purple.

desc: Cactus scale — armored scale specifically on prickly pear cactus (Opuntia). White circular waxy covers cluster on pads. Can weaken cactus.
hp: Specialist on cactus (cactus spp.). On Opuntia (cactus). Family Diaspididae.

desc: Oleander scale — circular flat armored scale found on oleander, olive, and many ornamentals. White waxy armor cover. Can weaken plants when heavy.
hp: Sessile sap feeder — honeydew producer. On olive.

desc: Splendid stink bug — colorful predatory stink bug. Feeds on beetle larvae — important predator of Colorado potato beetle. Beneficial.
hp: Solanaceae pest — feeds on potato, eggplant, or nightshade foliage/tubers.

desc: Bailey's acacia psyllid — tiny psyllid on Acacia baileyana (Cootamundra wattle). Introduced from Australia with its host.
hp: Specialist on Acacia baileyana (Cootamundra wattle). Introduced from Australia with host plant.

desc: Common alder aphid — tiny aphid on alder leaves. Introduced from Europe. Produces honeydew attracting sooty mold.
hp: On alder (alnus) in riparian habitat. Associated with White Alder. On Alnus (alder). Tiny.

desc: Strawberry tree aphid — green aphid on arbutus (strawberry tree). Introduced from Mediterranean. On ornamental plantings.
hp: Aphid predator. Important biocontrol agent. Specialist aphid predator — important biocontrol. On strawberr.

desc: American cochineal — scale insect producing carmine red dye. On prickly pear cactus. Historical source of red pigment for textiles and food coloring.

fm.Color: White waxy coating; crushed = brilliant red dye
fm.Note: Source of carmine red dye — historic

hp: Specialist on cactus (cactus spp.). On Opuntia (cactus). Family Dactylopiidae.

desc: Spiny pirate bug — tiny predatory anthocorid with spiny body. Feeds on thrips and mites. Beneficial predator in gardens.
hp: Predatory — hunts arthropod prey. Beneficial in gardens. Preys on thrips.

desc: Intermediate plant bug — small pale mirid on various plants. Feeds on plant sap. Part of the staggeringly diverse Miridae — the largest heteropteran family. Many mirid species await description from California. Requires microscopic examination of male genitalia for firm ID.
hp: Plant sap feeder on various hosts. Preys on ant.

desc: Mesquite psyllid — tiny psyllid on mesquite trees. Native. Can cause leaf distortion. Part of desert plant-insect community.
hp: On mesquite (prosopis) in desert habitat. On Prosopis (mesquite). Tiny.

desc: Slender gyponana — medium leafhopper with elongated body. On various trees. Part of the diverse canopy leafhopper community.
hp: On various trees. Large-bodied canopy leafhopper. Part of the diverse woodland canopy community.

desc: California scale-eating ladybug — tiny coccinellid that preys on scale insects. Classified in both Coccinellidae and historically associated with Hemiptera due to its prey.
hp: Predator of scale insects — biocontrol in orchards and gardens. Preys on scale insect. Tiny.

desc: Dear plant bug — small mirid on various plants. Named from Greek synecdoche (literary device) — the common name is a literal translation.
hp: On various plants. Small omnivorous mirid feeding on sap and small arthropods.

desc: Nesting whitefly — tiny whitefly building nest-like waxy structures on leaf undersides. Unusual nesting behavior among whiteflies. On citrus and ornamentals.
hp: Sap feeder on Citrus. Produces honeydew promoting sooty mold. Agricultural pest. On Citrus (citrus). Nest associate.

desc: Twin-spotted leafhopper — small leafhopper with diagnostic paired dark spots on vertex. On various plants. Can vector aster yellows and other phytoplasma diseases to crops and ornamentals. Important as a disease vector despite small size and inconspicuous appearance.
hp: On Asteraceae (composites) — daisies, sunflowers, sagebrush. Preys on ant. Family Cicadellidae.

desc: False indigo plant bug — small mirid specifically associated with false indigo (Amorpha californica). A native bug on a native plant — part of the evolved community of insects dependent on California's endemic shrubs. Host-specific — not found on other plants.
hp: Sap-feeding hemipteran on host plant tissue. Preys on ant. Family Miridae. On California.

desc: Buckwheat plant bug — small mirid associated with buckwheat (Eriogonum). Host-specific. Important component of buckwheat insect community.
hp: On california buckwheat (eriogonum). On Eriogonum (buckwheat). Family Miridae.

desc: Worm-marked leafhopper — small leafhopper with diagnostic vermiculate (worm-like, irregular wavy) markings on forewings. On oaks. Part of the diverse canopy leafhopper community — oak trees in LA support dozens of leafhopper species, each with different feeding niches.
hp: On coast live oak (quercus agrifolia). On Quercus (oak). Family Cicadellidae. Part of the diverse canopy leafhopper community .

desc: Magnificent cicada — medium-large cicada. Males produce loud buzzing song. One of the more impressive California cicadas.
hp: Root-feeding nymph underground for years. Adult male produces loud buzzing song from trees.

desc: Variegated leafhopper — small mottled leafhopper. Vectors phytoplasma diseases of various crops and ornamentals.
hp: Variegated leafhopper — vectors aster yellows and other phytoplasma diseases to crops and ornamentals. Important agricultural disease vector.

desc: Wounded cicada — medium cicada with reddish wing veins suggesting 'wounds.' Males sing loudly from shrubs. Foothill chaparral.
hp: Native chaparral scrub habitat. Root-feeding nymph underground. Family Cicadidae. Wounded.

desc: Brilliant plant bug — small shiny predatory mirid. Important predator of aphids and mites. Beneficial in gardens.
hp: Aphid predator — important biocontrol agent. Beneficial in gardens. Preys on aphid. Family Miridae.

desc: Angular plant bug — small green mirid common on willows and other riparian trees. Named for the angular shape of the cuneus (wing tip). Feeds on plant sap. Native. Common in riparian habitats throughout LA County. Part of the dominant willow-associated insect community.
hp: On willows (salix) in riparian habitat. On Salix (willow). Family Miridae.

desc: Shoshone shield bug — large scutellerid with shield covering entire back. On grasses. Impressive full-body shield shape.
hp: On grasses and forbs in grassland. On Poaceae (grasses).

desc: Similar wing-clapper — small cicada that produces clicking sounds by wing-clapping rather than typical cicada buzzing. Unusual sound production.

fm.Note: Clicks wings instead of buzzing — unusual

hp: Root-feeding nymph underground. Adult produces clicking sounds by wing-clapping, not tymbal vibration.

desc: Frontal planthopper — tiny delphacid on grasses. Part of the species-rich but poorly known grass-feeding planthopper community. Hundreds of species feed on grasses but are too small for casual observation. Important in grassland food webs as prey for spiders and birds.
hp: On grasses and forbs in grassland. Important prey for vertebrates.

desc: Spiny purshia psyllid — tiny psyllid on native shrubs. Part of the diverse California psyllid fauna — the state harbors hundreds of psyllid species, many undescribed, each typically associated with a specific host plant genus. Contributes to native plant-insect community dynamics.
hp: Sap-feeding hemipteran on host plant tissue. Preys on ant. Spiny/armed.

desc: Hoffman's backswimmer — aquatic bug swimming upside-down, using long hind legs as oars. Air bubble on ventral surface provides buoyancy and oxygen supply. Predatory — catches mosquito larvae, small tadpoles, and aquatic insects. Can bite painfully if handled carelessly.
hp: Predator of mosquito larvae — natural mosquito biocontrol. Preys on mosquito. Aquatic bug swimming upside-down, using long hind legs as oa.

desc: Ceanothus plant bug — green mirid specific to ceanothus (California lilac). Native bug on a native shrub. Part of diverse ceanothus insect community.
hp: On ceanothus (california lilac) — native shrub specialist. On Ceanothus (ceanothus). Family Miridae.

desc: Gill's whitefly — tiny whitefly on native trees. Part of the diverse California whitefly fauna — dozens of native species, each typically host-specific. Distinguished from pest whiteflies by host plant (native trees, not crops). A component of native plant-insect food webs.
hp: Ecological role from description context. Preys on whitefl.

desc: Briggs's barklouse — small psocid living gregariously on tree bark. Feeds on algae, lichens, and fungal spores growing on bark surfaces. Not parasitic — a harmless decomposer. Lives in herds of dozens on tree trunks. Introduced from Europe. Harmless to trees.
hp: Detritivore — breaks down dead organic matter. Important in nutrient cycling.

desc: Single-spotted barklouse — small psocid with diagnostic single dark wing spot. On tree bark in herds. Feeds on bark microflora — algae, fungal spores, and lichen fragments. Harmless to trees. The single wing spot distinguishes it from other bark-dwelling psocids.
hp: Feeds on lichens and bark microflora. Family Mesopsocidae.

desc: Glass-winged barklouse — small psocid with clear (hyaline), transparent wings. On tree bark feeding on algae, lichens, and fungal growth. The transparent wings make it nearly invisible on bark. Gregarious — forms small herds. European introduction. Harmless decomposer.
hp: Detritivore — breaks down dead organic matter. Important in nutrient cycling. Family Elipsocidae.

desc: Spined assassin bug — medium assassin bug with crown-like spines on pronotum. Important predator of pest caterpillars and beetles. Can bite if handled.
hp: Pine; caterpillar predator. Preys on caterpillars.

desc: Long-necked seed bug — distinctive seed bug with extremely elongated head. Fast runner. Under debris. Unique long head is diagnostic.
hp: LA County true bug — habitat-specific ecological role. Elongated.

desc: Manzanita ambush bug — tiny ambush bug hiding in manzanita flowers. Raptorial forelegs grab prey much larger than itself.
hp: On Arctostaphylos (manzanita) in chaparral. Native shrub specialist. Tiny.

desc: Common damsel bug — medium predatory bug. Important biocontrol agent — catches aphids, caterpillars, and other pests. Slender raptorial forelegs.
hp: Biocontrol agent; aphid predator. Specialist aphid predator — important biocontrol. Preys on aphid. Family Nabidae.

desc: Pear sucker — tiny psyllid on pear trees. Major pest causing pear decline disease. Honeydew production attracts sooty mold. Important orchard pest.
hp: Plant/animal disease vector. Agricultural/garden pest. Preys on ant.

desc: Japanese maple leafhopper — small pale leafhopper introduced from Asia. On Japanese maples and other ornamentals. Can cause stippling.
hp: On Japanese maples and other Acer. Introduced from Asia. Can damage ornamental maples.

desc: Mulsant's water treader — tiny bug that walks on water surface film. Semi-aquatic. Predatory on mosquito larvae and small insects trapped in surface tension.
hp: Predator of mosquito larvae — natural mosquito biocontrol. Preys on mosquito. Tiny bug that walks on water surface film. Semi-aquatic. Pre.

desc: Depicted seed bug — small dark seed bug with pale chevron markings. Under debris on ground. Feeds on fallen seeds. Dry habitats.
hp: Ground-dwelling seed feeder — on fallen seeds under litter. Seed predation affects plant regeneration. Feeds on fallen seeds.

desc: Foxglove aphid — pale green aphid with dark knee spots (diagnostic). On potatoes, lettuce, foxglove, and many plants. Vectors plant viruses.
hp: Aphid predator. Important biocontrol agent. Specialist aphid predator — important biocontrol. On potato.

desc: Elegant leafhopper — small leafhopper with refined pale markings on brown body. On various shrubs in chaparral and coastal sage scrub. Part of the large genus Scaphytopius — a primarily New World leafhopper genus. Can vector phytoplasma diseases of native plants.
hp: Coastal sage scrub and chaparral. Associated with Black Sage. Preys on ant.

desc: Reddish plant bug — small mirid with distinctive reddish-brown tinge. On various native plants. Part of the neotropical mirid radiation in western NA.
hp: On various native plants. Small reddish-brown mirid. Part of native chaparral bug community.

desc: Leafcurl plum aphid — green aphid causing severe leaf curling on plum trees. Migrates to composites in summer. Can distort plum shoots.
hp: Aphid predator. Important biocontrol agent. Specialist aphid predator — important biocontrol. On Asteraceae (composites).

desc: Hakea psyllid — tiny psyllid introduced from Australia with its host plant hakea. On ornamental Hakea plantings in LA gardens. Causes leaf curling and stunting. An example of a specialist herbivore following its host plant into cultivation. Not established on native plants.
hp: Sap-feeding hemipteran on host plant tissue. Preys on ant. Tiny.

desc: Black olive scale — dark hemispherical scale with H-shaped ridge on back. On olive, citrus, and ornamentals. Important olive pest. H-mark diagnostic.

fm.Color: Dark; H-shaped ridge on back — diagnostic

hp: Black scale — major citrus and olive pest. Hemispherical black scale on twigs. Honeydew and sooty mold. Biocontrol by Metaphycus helvolus.

desc: Yucca plant bug — small dark mirid specialized on yucca plants. Can cause stippling damage (tiny pale feeding scars) on yucca leaves. Native bug on a native plant — an expected component of the yucca insect community. Named 'valida' (strong) for robust body.
hp: Specialist on yucca (yucca spp.). On Yucca (yucca). Robust.

desc: Yucca sharpshooter — green sharpshooter associated with yucca. Part of the diverse sharpshooter fauna. Can vector Xylella.
hp: Specialist on yucca (yucca spp.). On Yucca (yucca). Family Cicadellidae.

desc: Beautiful plant bug — tiny predatory mirid. Despite its small size, an important biocontrol agent — feeds on whiteflies, spider mites, and other small pest arthropods. Both an herbivore and predator (omnivorous mirid). Commercially considered for greenhouse biocontrol.
hp: Biocontrol agent; spider predator. Preys on whiteflies. Biocontrol agent. Tiny.

desc: Yerba santa lace bug — tiny tingid with delicate lace-patterned wings, specific to yerba santa (Eriodictyon). Host-specific — one of many native lace bugs each tied to a particular plant genus. The exquisite wing venation creates a true lace pattern visible only under magnification.
hp: Specialist on yerba santa (Eriodictyon). Host-specific native lace bug on a native medicinal plant.

desc: Wrinkle-winged leafhopper — small dark leafhopper with diagnostic rugose (wrinkled) forewing surface texture. Nocturnal — comes to lights. On oaks. The wrinkled wing surface is unusual among leafhoppers, which typically have smooth wings.
hp: On coast live oak (quercus agrifolia). Associated with Coast Live Oak. On Quercus (oak). Family Cicadellidae.

desc: Sand-dwelling seed bug — small dark seed bug introduced from Europe. Under debris in sandy habitats. Expanding in SoCal.
hp: LA County true bug — habitat-specific ecological role.

desc: Mulberry whitefly — tiny whitefly on mulberry trees. Can cause sooty mold from honeydew production. On ornamental mulberries.
hp: Specialist on mulberry (Morus). Can cause sooty mold from honeydew on leaves.

desc: Manzanita whitefly — tiny whitefly on manzanita (Arctostaphylos) leaf undersides. Native species on a native host. Produces waxy filaments.
hp: On Arctostaphylos (manzanita) in chaparral. Native shrub specialist. Tiny.

desc: Leafcurl ash aphid — woolly aphid causing severe leaf curling on ash trees. White waxy secretion. Can distort and drop ash leaves.
hp: Aphid predator. Important biocontrol agent. Specialist aphid predator — important biocontrol. On Fraxinus (ash).

desc: California psyllid — tiny jumping plant louse on native plants. Part of the diverse California psyllid fauna — the state hosts an extraordinary radiation of native psyllids, many still undescribed. Host-plant specific. Distinguished from pest psyllids by native plant association.
hp: Sap-feeding hemipteran on host plant tissue. Preys on ant. Tiny.

desc: Flannelbush psyllid — tiny psyllid on flannelbush (Fremontodendron). Native specialist on a native shrub. Forms galls on leaves.
hp: Specialist on flannelbush (Fremontodendron). Native psyllid on an iconic native shrub.

desc: Gelatinous whitefly — tiny whitefly producing diagnostic gelatinous, glass-like waxy coverings on leaf undersides. On various trees. Distinguished from other whiteflies by the transparent, jelly-like pupal case. Part of LA's diverse native whitefly assemblage.
hp: On various native trees. Produces diagnostic gelatinous waxy pupal coverings on leaf undersides.

desc: Bougainvillea mealybug — white waxy mealybug on bougainvillea and other ornamentals. Can cause leaf distortion and drop. Honeydew attracts sooty mold.
hp: On bougainvillea and ornamentals. Waxy mealybug producing honeydew and sooty mold.

desc: Stuffed plant bug — small mirid on native vegetation. Named for Mexican entomologist. Part of the diverse neotropical mirid fauna reaching into SoCal.
hp: On native vegetation. Part of the diverse native California mirid fauna.

desc: Spiny lace bug — tiny lace bug with pronounced pronotal spines. On various shrubs. Creates pale stippling on leaf upper surface from feeding.
hp: In conifer forests (pinus). Associated with Coulter Pine. On Pinus (pine). Spiny/armed.

desc: Essig's ceanothus bug — small green mirid on ceanothus (California lilac). Named for Edward O. Essig, pioneering California entomologist at UC Berkeley. One of several Ceanothia species — a genus of mirids endemic to California, each associated with ceanothus species.
hp: On ceanothus (california lilac) — native shrub specialist. On Ceanothus (ceanothus). Family Miridae.

desc: Creosote lac scale — scale insect producing lac (resin) on creosote bush stems. Native. Creates conspicuous dark encrustations on creosote branches.
hp: Specialist on creosote bush (larrea tridentata). On Larrea (creosote). Family Kerriidae.

desc: Dwarf leafhopper — very small (3mm) leafhopper, among the smallest in LA County. On various plants. Despite tiny size, can vector phytoplasma diseases. Part of the extremely species-rich leafhopper family Cicadellidae — over 22,000 species worldwide.
hp: Dwarf leafhopper — one of smallest NA leafhoppers (3mm). Despite tiny size, can vector phytoplasma diseases. On various plants.

desc: Golden-streaked leafhopper — small leafhopper with attractive golden streaks on pale green body. On birch and alder. The golden markings are distinctive — most leafhoppers in the canopy community are plain green or brown. Part of the birch-associated insect community.
hp: On alder (alnus) in riparian habitat. Associated with White Alder. On Alnus (alder). Family Cicadellidae.

desc: Red-written plant bug — mirid with fine reddish markings on wings ('rufoscriptus' = red-written). On oaks and other trees. Partly predatory.
hp: Predatory hemipteran — captures other insects with raptorial forelegs. On Quercus (oak).

desc: Minute ceanothus bug — the smallest of the Ceanothia plant bugs, barely 3mm. On ceanothus (California lilac). Ceanothia is a California-endemic mirid genus — found nowhere else on Earth — with each species associated with specific Ceanothus species. A micro-endemic specialist.
hp: On ceanothus (california lilac) — native shrub specialist. On Ceanothus (ceanothus). Family Miridae (3mm).

desc: Ringed booklouse — tiny (1-2mm) wingless psocid found indoors in damp conditions. Feeds on mold, fungal spores, and starchy materials including book bindings. Not a true louse — does not bite humans. Indicator of excess indoor humidity. Can damage stored documents.
hp: Ringed booklouse — indoor psocid on mold and book bindings. Not a true louse. Indicator of damp conditions. Can damage stored documents.

desc: Black bean aphid — dark aphid forming dense colonies on broad beans, chard, and ornamentals. Major garden pest. Complex host-alternating life cycle.
hp: Legume associate — on beans, peas, or other Fabaceae. Preys on aphid.

desc: Barbara's barklouse — small psocid found on tree bark. Feeds on microflora — algae, lichens, and fungal spores growing on bark surfaces.
hp: Feeds on lichens and bark microflora. Family Psocidae.

desc: Robust barklouse — larger than most psocids. On tree bark feeding on algae and lichen microflora. One of the more conspicuous barklice.
hp: Feeds on lichens and bark microflora. Robust.

desc: Related rough stink bug — large bark-colored stink bug. Feeds on tree fruits. Bark-cryptic when resting on tree trunks. Rough textured.
hp: Sap-feeding hemipteran on host plant tissue. Cryptic.

desc: Western bat bug — ectoparasite of bats related to bed bugs. In bat roosts. Can temporarily bite humans when bats are excluded from buildings.
hp: Western bat bug — ectoparasite of bats, closely related to bed bug. In bat roosts. Can bite humans when bats excluded from buildings.

desc: Waterlily aphid — dark aphid on waterlilies and aquatic plants. One of few truly aquatic aphids. Can damage ornamental pond plants.

fm.Color: Dark; on waterlilies and aquatic plants

hp: Freshwater aquatic habitat — streams and ponds. Preys on aphid.

desc: Oak lace bug — tiny bug with intricate lace wing pattern on oak leaf undersides. Causes stippling damage. Beautiful under magnification.

fm.Color: White lace pattern; on oak leaves

hp: On coast live oak (quercus agrifolia). On Quercus (oak). Tiny.

desc: Dorsal scentless plant bug — small pale rhopalid with darker dorsal stripe. On various weedy plants. Unlike stink bugs, lacks strong defensive odor.
hp: On various weedy plants. Scentless plant bug — lacks defensive stink glands. Dorsal stripe.

desc: Pale-bordered stink bug — medium green-brown pentatomid with distinctive pale lateral edges. On various plants — generalist feeder. Distinguished from Euschistus by paler margins and slightly different scutellum shape. Common but frequently misidentified. Native.
hp: Plant sap feeder on various hosts. Family Pentatomidae.

desc: Half-banded seed bug — small dark rhyparochromid with partial pale crossband on forewings. Under bark and debris. Feeds on fallen seeds and detritus. Predatory on small invertebrates as well. Litter-dwelling community. Nocturnal. Native to western NA.
hp: Ground-dwelling seed feeder — on fallen seeds under litter. Seed predation affects plant regeneration.

desc: Bald stink bug — small green pentatomid. 'Calva' (bald) refers to the smooth, unornamented scutellum. Feeds on fruit and developing seeds of various plants. Generalist herbivore. Distinguished from other green stink bugs by the smooth, unpunctured scutellar surface.
hp: Plant sap feeder on various hosts. Family Pentatomidae.

desc: Antigua plant bug — small dark mirid introduced from Central America, now established in SoCal gardens. On various ornamental plants. Part of the growing list of Neotropical mirid introductions reaching LA — likely via nursery trade in ornamental plants.
hp: Sap-feeding hemipteran on host plant tissue. Preys on ant. Family Miridae. Small dark mirid introduced from Central America, now establ.

desc: American blue bug — small metallic blue predatory stink bug. Feeds on flea beetles and other chrysomelids. Beneficial. One of the most colorful pentatomids.

fm.Color: Metallic blue; predatory on flea beetles

hp: On beets, chard, or spinach (Chenopodiaceae/Amaranthaceae). Metallic. Small metallic blue predatory stink bug. Feeds on flea beetl.

desc: Blackberry-cereal aphid — green aphid on cereals and grasses, also colonizing Rubus (blackberry). Important crop pest capable of transmitting barley yellow dwarf virus. Alternate hosts include strawberry and grasses. Part of the economically important grain aphid complex.
hp: Aphid alternating between Rubus (blackberry) and cereal grasses. Crop virus vector.

desc: Coreopsis aphid — small dark aphid on coreopsis and related composites. Can build dense colonies on flower stems.
hp: Ambushes prey on flowers. Preys on aphids. On Asteraceae (composites).

desc: Brown soft scale — soft scale insect on citrus, ficus, and many ornamentals. Produces copious honeydew. Major nursery pest.
hp: Sap feeder on Citrus. Produces honeydew promoting sooty mold. Agricultural pest. On Ficus (fig).

desc: Green coffee scale — pale green soft scale on coffee, citrus, and ornamentals. Tropical origin. Produces honeydew.
hp: Green scale — soft scale on citrus, coffee, ornamentals. Produces copious honeydew promoting sooty mold. Important in tropical agriculture.

desc: Green shield scale — soft scale producing white cottony egg sac. On guava, citrus, and many ornamentals. Tropical pest established in SoCal.
hp: Green shield scale — on citrus and ornamentals. Produces white cottony egg sac beneath body. Honeydew attracts ants and promotes sooty mold.

desc: Dogwood-milkweed aphid — green aphid alternating between dogwood and milkweed. Complex seasonal host-switching life cycle.
hp: On milkweed (asclepias) — aposematic. Associated with Narrow-leaf Milkweed.

desc: Pendulous plant bug — small mirid on native vegetation in chaparral. Part of the diverse but poorly studied native plant bug fauna.
hp: Native chaparral scrub habitat. Preys on ant. Family Miridae. Small mirid on native vegetation in chaparral. Part of the d.

desc: Delicate plant bug — very small, fragile mirid of coastal sage scrub and chaparral. On native Asteraceae. Feeds on plant sap and small arthropods. Part of a primarily Neotropical genus represented in LA by this single delicate species at its northern range limit.
hp: Coastal sage scrub and chaparral. Associated with Black Sage. On Asteraceae (composites). Family Miridae.

desc: Branching plant bug — small green-brown mirid on various trees and shrubs. Part of the enormous genus Phytocoris — over 500 species worldwide. Most are nocturnal and come to lights. Both herbivorous and predatory — feeds on plant sap and small arthropods.
hp: Predatory hemipteran — captures other insects with raptorial forelegs. Preys on ant. Enormous.

desc: Sowthistle aphid — green aphid alternating between currant (winter) and sowthistle (summer). Vectors lettuce necrotic yellows virus.
hp: Aphid predator. Important biocontrol agent. Specialist aphid predator — important biocontrol. On lettuce.

desc: European fruit scale — brown convex scale on many fruit and ornamental trees. Introduced from Europe. Produces honeydew attracting sooty mold.
hp: Sessile sap feeder — honeydew producer. Family Coccidae.

desc: Black-spotted bamboo aphid — tiny pale aphid with dark spots on bamboo. Introduced from Asia. Can coat bamboo leaves with honeydew.

fm.Color: Pale with dark spots; on bamboo

hp: Colonial sap feeder producing honeydew. Preys on aphid. Tiny.

desc: Spotted alfalfa aphid — tiny yellow aphid with dark dorsal spots. Major pest of alfalfa — can devastate stands. Injects toxic saliva.
hp: Aphid predator. Important biocontrol agent. Specialist aphid predator — important biocontrol. On alfalfa. Tiny.

desc: Paddock's evening bug — small mirid active at dusk. Named for collector Paddock. On various plants in chaparral. Part of the poorly known evening-active mirid fauna.
hp: Native chaparral scrub habitat. Family Miridae.

desc: California plant bug — small green mirid endemic to California. On native shrubs in chaparral and coastal sage scrub. Feeds on plant sap. Part of the California-endemic component of the mirid fauna — several genera are found nowhere else. Conservation-relevant by endemism.
hp: Coastal sage scrub and chaparral. Associated with Black Sage. Preys on ant. Family Miridae.

desc: Behrens's treehopper — small brown membracid on oaks. Named for James Behrens, early California naturalist. Distinctive pronotal 'horn' — treehoppers are famous for extravagant pronotal sculptures, though this species is relatively modest. On twigs. Honeydew attracts ants.
hp: On coast live oak (quercus agrifolia). On Quercus (oak). Family Membracidae.

desc: Swallow nest bug — blood-feeding bug living in cliff swallow nests. Related to bed bugs. Can enter homes when swallows nest on buildings.
hp: Obligate human blood parasite. Nocturnal feeder hiding in mattress seams. Nest associate.

desc: White sage aphid — aphid on white sage (Artemisia ludoviciana) and related sagebrush. Native aphid on native plants.
hp: In coastal sage scrub habitat. Associated with California Sagebrush. On Artemisia (sagebrush). Family Aphididae.

desc: California willow aphid — green aphid on willows. Native species. Part of the diverse California aphid fauna — the state hosts hundreds of native aphid species, each typically host-specific. Distinguished from introduced aphids by native host association.
hp: On willows (salix) in riparian habitat. On Salix (willow). Family Aphididae.

desc: Blood-red psyllid — tiny psyllid with distinctive reddish-orange coloring. On native shrubs. One of the more colorful psyllid species.
hp: Pear psyllid — NOT a blood feeder (mislabeled). Tiny sap-feeding psyllid on Pyrus (pear). Can vector pear decline phytoplasma.

desc: English grain aphid — green aphid on wheat, barley, and other cereals. Major crop pest. Vectors barley yellow dwarf virus.
hp: Stored product pest of grain and dried foods. On wheat. Family Aphididae.

desc: Lined spittlebug — small green froghopper. Nymphs produce frothy spittle masses on plant stems. Adults jump powerfully.
hp: Nymphs produce frothy spittle masses on plant stems — protective foam shelters. Adults free-living.

desc: Protean shield bug — small scutellerid with highly variable coloring and pattern (hence 'proteus,' the shape-shifting Greek god). Shield covers entire abdomen. On grasses. Distinguished from stink bugs by the greatly enlarged scutellum covering the entire dorsum.
hp: On grasses and forbs in grassland. On Poaceae (grasses).

desc: Beautiful shore bug — small dark saldid found at water margins. Predatory. Jumps and flies short distances when disturbed.
hp: Predatory hemipteran — captures other insects with raptorial forelegs. Family Saldidae.

desc: Broom aphid — aphid on broom (Cytisus, Genista) and related legumes. Introduced from Europe with ornamental broom plantings. Can cause shoot distortion and honeydew accumulation on ornamental shrubs. Part of the large influx of European aphids following ornamental plant trade.
hp: Colonial sap feeder producing honeydew. On Fabaceae (legumes).

desc: Desert bee assassin — large red-and-black assassin bug of desert habitats. Ambushes pollinators on flowers. Related to California bee assassin.
hp: Flower-ambush predator — captures pollinating insects on flower heads.

desc: Ash-scrub plant bug — small mirid associated with sagebrush (Artemisia) and related Asteraceae shrubs. Host-specific. Chaparral habitat.
hp: Coastal sage scrub and chaparral.

desc: Black-banded stink bug — small stink bug with black and reddish-orange banding. On various plants. Related to C. conspicillaris.
hp: On various plants. Related to C. Conspicillaris. Black-and-orange banded stink bug.

desc: Red date scale — tiny red-colored scale insect on date palm fronds. Can damage date production. Named for entomologist C.L. Marlatt.
hp: Predator of scale insects — biocontrol in orchards and gardens. Preys on scale insect. Tiny.

desc: Dorsal plant bug — small mirid with darker dorsal markings. On various plants. Both herbivorous and predatory on small arthropods.
hp: Predatory hemipteran — captures other insects with raptorial forelegs. Preys on ant. Family Miridae.

desc: Guava aphid — dark aphid with long cornicles on guava and related plants. Introduced from Asia. On ornamental Myrtaceae.
hp: Aphid predator. Important biocontrol agent. Specialist aphid predator — important biocontrol. On corn.

desc: Shining green tarragon aphid — green glossy aphid on Artemisia (sagebrush/tarragon). Unusually shiny for an aphid.
hp: In coastal sage scrub habitat. Associated with California Sagebrush. On Artemisia (sagebrush). Family Aphididae.

desc: Nichol's treehopper — small dark brown membracid resembling a thorn on a twig. The dark coloring suggests a tuxedo (hence the common name). On various trees and shrubs. Honeydew production attracts ants that protect the treehopper from predators in exchange.
hp: Predatory hemipteran — captures other insects with raptorial forelegs. Family Membracidae.

desc: Shield-faced planthopper — small pale green acanaloniid. Broad flat body resembles a leaf fragment. On various trees and shrubs.
hp: Sap feeder on shrubs. Broad flat body mimics a leaf fragment. Produces flocculent wax.

desc: Caldwell's delphacid — tiny planthopper on grasses. Part of the diverse but poorly known Delphacidae — one of the largest families of planthoppers, with hundreds of grass-feeding species. Too small for casual observation but collectively important herbivores in grassland ecosystems.
hp: Grass sap-feeder (Poaceae). Part of grassland herbivore community. On Poaceae (grasses). Tiny.

desc: Boheman's seed bug — small dark seed bug under debris. Named for Swedish entomologist Carl Boheman. Feeds on fallen seeds in dry habitats.
hp: Plant sap feeder on various hosts. Family Rhyparochromidae.

desc: Green triozid — tiny bright green psyllid on various plants. Can cause leaf curling. Part of the diverse psyllid fauna of western NA.
hp: On various plants. Tiny green psyllid — can cause leaf curling. Part of diverse native psyllid fauna.

desc: Intermediate plant bug — small green mirid of intermediate size within its genus. On various shrubs. Feeds on plant sap.
hp: Plant sap feeder on various hosts. Preys on ant. Family Miridae.

desc: Agile plant bug — small, quick-running mirid. Both herbivorous (sap-feeding) and predatory on mites, aphids, and whitefly eggs. Important biocontrol agent in tomato greenhouses — commercially released in Europe for whitefly management. On solanaceous plants in LA gardens.
hp: Biocontrol agent; aphid predator. Specialist aphid predator — important biocontrol. On tomato.

desc: Romantic leafhopper — small pale leafhopper. One of the more poetically named insects in the guide. On various plants in chaparral. The genus Neocoelidia is primarily New World. Distinguished by male genitalia — like many leafhoppers, external characters alone cannot confirm identity.
hp: Chaparral sap feeder — on native shrubs. Part of native plant-insect community. Family Cicadellidae. Romantic.

desc: Zigzag treehopper — tiny treehopper with zigzag horn on pronotum. On oaks. Part of the diverse treehopper fauna.
hp: On coast live oak (quercus agrifolia). On Quercus (oak). Tiny.

desc: Oman's seed bug — small dark rhyparochromid found under debris in dry habitats. Feeds on fallen seeds of native plants. Named for P.W. Oman, prominent 20th-century hemipterist. Part of the diverse but poorly known ground-dwelling seed bug community in California chaparral.
hp: Under debris on ground. Chaparral habitat.

desc: Vendor's plant bug — small green mirid. Part of the enormous family Miridae — the largest family of Heteroptera with over 11,000 described species. Feeds on plant sap. On native chaparral vegetation. Most mirids are too small and cryptic for non-specialists to notice.
hp: Sap-feeding hemipteran on host plant tissue. Preys on ant. Chaparral. Feeds on plant sap.

desc: Atriplex ensign scale — scale insect with white waxy plates arranged like flags/ensigns. On saltbush (Atriplex). Native.
hp: Predator of scale insects — biocontrol in orchards and gardens. Preys on scale insect. Family Ortheziidae.

desc: Tubercle-legged burrowing bug — small dark cydnid with bumpy legs. Lives in soil around plant roots. Feeds on root sap.
hp: Plant sap feeder on various hosts. Preys on ant. Soil-dwelling.

desc: Black seed bug — small black lygaeid under debris in dry habitats. Feeds on fallen seeds. Distinguished from other dark seed bugs by uniformly jet-black coloring. In coastal sage scrub and chaparral — under rocks and leaf litter in arid microhabitats.
hp: Coastal sage scrub and chaparral. Associated with Black Sage. Family Rhyparochromidae.

desc: Marked plant bug — small mirid with bold dark markings on pale green body. On native Asteraceae and other plants in coastal sage scrub and chaparral. Plant sap feeder. Part of the enormous family Miridae — over 11,000 species worldwide, the largest family of true bugs.
hp: Coastal sage scrub and chaparral. Associated with Black Sage. On Asteraceae (composites).

desc: Red-veined scentless plant bug — pale rhopalid with distinctive reddish wing veins (hence 'rubrovenosus'). On various plants in dry habitats. Scentless plant bugs (Rhopalidae) resemble small coreids but lack the scent glands. Feeds on seeds and developing fruit.
hp: Plant sap feeder on various hosts. Preys on ant. Family Rhopalidae.

desc: European elm scale — cottony scale on elms producing white waxy secretion. Introduced from Europe. Can weaken elm trees.

fm.Color: Brown with white waxy fringe; on elms

hp: Sessile sap feeder — honeydew producer. On cotton. Family Eriococcidae.

desc: Coquillett's barklouse — small psocid on tree bark. Named for pioneering dipterist Daniel W. Coquillett of the USDA. Feeds on bark microflora.
hp: On tree bark. Part of bark-dwelling community. Family Psocidae.

desc: Manzanita scale — circular armored scale on manzanita (Arctostaphylos) bark. White waxy cover protects the sessile female underneath. Native species on a native host — part of the naturally occurring insect community on chaparral shrubs. Not a pest in natural settings.

fm.Color: White waxy cover on manzanita bark

hp: On Arctostaphylos (manzanita) in chaparral. Native shrub specialist. Family Diaspididae.

desc: Cycad scale — white armored scale on cycad leaves. Can damage ornamental cycads and sagos. Introduced. White waxy covers on fronds.
hp: Sessile sap feeder — honeydew producer. Family Diaspididae.

desc: Oak scale — tiny armored scale on oak bark and twigs. Circular white waxy cover. Native species on native oaks. Usually not damaging.

fm.Color: Tiny white circular; on oak bark

hp: On oak bark. Part of bark-dwelling arthropod community. On Quercus (oak). Tiny.

desc: Pine needle scale — white elongated armored scale on pine needles. Can cause yellowing and needle drop. White waxy covers conspicuous on green needles.

fm.Color: White elongated waxy cover on pine needles

hp: Sap-feeding hemipteran on host plant tissue. On Pinus (pine).
